framework.name: QNRCS-2026.1
framework.description: National Cybersecurity Reference Framework (QNRCS) — version 2026.1, published by the National Cybersecurity Centre (CNCS). Organises cybersecurity measures into 6 domains (Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond and Recover) with 4 maturity levels: Basic, Substantial, High and Advanced.

PR.GA.name: Identity, Authentication and Access Control Management

qnrcs-basico.name: Basic Level
qnrcs-basico.description: Profile applicable to entities with minimum cybersecurity obligations under the NIS2 Directive. Requires implementation of the Basic level defined in the QNRCS.

qnrcs-substancial.name: Substantial Level
qnrcs-substancial.description: Profile applicable to important entities (Annex II of the NIS2 Directive). Requires cumulative implementation of the Basic and Substantial levels defined in the QNRCS.

qnrcs-elevado.name: High Level
qnrcs-elevado.description: Profile applicable to essential entities (Annex I of the NIS2 Directive). Requires cumulative implementation of the Basic, Substantial and High levels defined in the QNRCS.

GR.CO-1.title: The entity's mission, vision, risk management strategy and objectives are defined and communicated
GR.CO-1.description: The company must have written and shared its mission, vision and objectives, including how it plans to deal with cybersecurity risks. It is the starting point for aligning the entire organization.

2.description: Define, record, communicate and prioritize the entity's mission, vision, risk management strategy and objectives, including cybersecurity priorities.
indicators: Write an internal document with the company's mission, vision, risk management strategy and objectives, including a section dedicated to cybersecurity priorities.
Submit the document for formal approval by management or administration.
Communicate the approved document to all staff (by internal email, general meeting or publication on the intranet).
Record the date of communication and the recipients who received the document.
evidence: PDF document with sections: Mission | Vision | Risk management strategy | Objectives | Cybersecurity priorities, signed/approved by management, with approval date.
Email or meeting minutes with date and list of recipients proving communication to staff.
2.tip: Use a simple 2-3 page document. It doesn't need to be extensive: the important thing is that it exists, is approved and that everyone knows where to find it.
2.effort: 4–6 hours | One person (with management support) | No additional cost

GR.CO-2.title: The needs and expectations of internal and external stakeholders are understood and reflected in the management of the entity's cybersecurity risks
GR.CO-2.description: The company identifies the people and organizations (internal and external) that are interested in or affected by its cybersecurity, and takes their needs into account in the way it manages risks.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: Identify relevant internal and external stakeholders, understand their cybersecurity needs and expectations, and reflect this information in the risk management strategy.
indicators: List all internal (e.g. management, IT staff, employees) and external (e.g. customers, suppliers, regulators, partners) stakeholders.
For each interested party, record their needs and expectations regarding the company's cybersecurity.
Check whether the cybersecurity risk management strategy already addresses these needs and expectations.
If there are gaps, propose adjustments to the strategy to cover them.
evidence: Excel sheet or PDF document with columns: Stakeholder | Type (internal/external) | Needs and expectations | Reflected in risk management (yes/no) | Observations, signed by the person responsible for cybersecurity.
Print or excerpt of the risk management strategy containing references to the identified stakeholders.
4.tip: Think of stakeholders as all the people or organizations that would be affected if the company suffered a cybersecurity incident: customers, suppliers, personnel, regulators.
4.effort: 3–5 hours | One person | No additional cost

GR.CO-3.title: Legal, regulatory and contractual requirements for cybersecurity are understood and managed
GR.CO-3.description: The company knows and complies with the laws, regulations and contractual obligations that apply to its cybersecurity, both at national and European level.

1.description: Identify and apply the legal and regulatory cybersecurity requirements applicable to the entity.
indicators: List all cybersecurity laws and regulations applicable to the company (e.g. GDPR, Cybersecurity Legal Framework (NIS2), sectoral regulations).
For each legal requirement, identify the concrete obligations that the company has to comply with.
Check whether the company is fulfilling each identified obligation.
Record the status of compliance and, if necessary, note the corrective actions to be taken.
evidence: Excel sheet with columns: Law/Regulation | Concrete obligation | Conformity status (compliant/non-compliant) | Corrective action (if applicable) | Responsible | Verification date, signed by the IT manager or legal advisor.
Copy of internal records or decisions that prove compliance with legal requirements (e.g. appointment of DPO, registration with the CNCS, contracts with security clauses).
1.tip: Start with GDPR and national cybersecurity legislation. The CNCS (National Cybersecurity Centre) website has practical guides on legal obligations for companies.
1.effort: 4–8 hours | One person (with legal support if necessary) | No additional cost

2.description: Substantial

GR.CO-4.title: The entity's critical assets and services that external stakeholders depend on are identified and communicated
GR.CO-4.description: The company identifies its most important services (those that customers, partners or other entities depend on) and communicates this information to external stakeholders.

1.description: Identify, document and communicate critical services that external stakeholders depend on, respecting the need-to-know principle.
indicators: List all the services that the company provides to external entities (customers, partners, suppliers).
Identify which of these services are critical (whose unavailability causes a significant impact on external parties).
Document the list of critical services indicating who depends on each one.
Communicate the identification of critical services to relevant external stakeholders, sharing only the necessary information.
evidence: PDF or Excel document with columns: Service | Description | Dependent External Stakeholder | Rating (critical/non-critical) | Identification date, signed by the person responsible for the area or management.
Email or letter sent to external interested parties communicating the identified critical services, with shipping date.
1.tip: Ask yourself: if this service stopped for 24 hours, who outside the company would be affected? If the answer is yes, it is probably a critical service.
1.effort: 3–5 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Classify, prioritize and integrate critical services into the risk management process, defining criteria for classification and prioritization.
indicators: Define formal criteria for classifying and prioritizing critical services (e.g. financial impact, number of dependents, level of exposure).
Apply the criteria to each critical service already identified and assign them a priority.
Integrate classified critical services into the existing risk management process, ensuring they are considered in risk assessments.
Register and approve the result with management.
evidence: PDF or Excel document with columns: Critical service | Classification criteria applied | Priority assigned | Integrated into risk management (yes/no) | Date, approved by management.
Print or excerpt of the risk management register where critical services are integrated.
2.tip: Use a simple scale of 1 to 3 (high, medium, low) for prioritization. Don't complicate things with extensive matrices; the objective is to have clear and applicable criteria.
2.effort: 3–5 hours | One person | No additional cost

GR.CO-5.title: The resilience requirements necessary to support the provision of critical services are defined
GR.CO-5.description: The company defines what is necessary to ensure that its critical services continue to function, even in the event of a failure or incident, including redundancies and recovery plans.

1.description: Identify and record the resilience requirements necessary to guarantee the support of critical services.
indicators: For each critical service identified, list the resources on which it depends (systems, people, suppliers, infrastructures).
For each resource, define what happens if it fails and what is the maximum acceptable time of unavailability.
Record the minimum resilience requirements for each critical service (e.g. backups, alternative equipment, supplier emergency contact).
Validate registration with the management or IT manager.
evidence: PDF or Excel document with columns: Critical service | Dependent resources | Maximum acceptable downtime | Resilience requirements defined | Responsible | Date, validated by the IT manager or management.
Registration of validation (signature or confirmation email) by management.
1.tip: Think of maximum downtime as the answer to the question: how long can I go without this service before the business is seriously impacted?
1.effort: 4–6 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Implement redundancy mechanisms for the availability of critical services. Define, approve and implement a Business Continuity Plan with recovery times.
indicators: For each critical service, implement at least one redundancy mechanism (e.g. secondary server, alternative internet connection, backup provider).
Write the Business Continuity Plan, defining for each critical service: recovery procedure, estimated recovery time and person responsible.
Submit the Business Continuity Plan for management approval.
Communicate the plan to relevant staff and ensure it is accessible in the event of an emergency.
evidence: Technical evidence of implemented redundancy mechanisms (e.g. secondary server configuration printout, alternative internet connection contract).
Business Continuity Plan in PDF with sections: Critical service | Recovery procedure | Set recovery time | Responsible, approved and signed by management, with date.
2.tip: Redundancy does not mean duplicating everything. For an SME, it can be as simple as having a second 4G/5G internet connection or having backups in a cloud service.
2.effort: 8–16 hours | One to two people | Variable cost (according to redundancies)

GR.CO-6.title: The external assets and services on which the entity depends are identified and communicated
GR.CO-6.description: The company identifies the essential external services on which it depends (e.g. internet provider, email service, cloud platform) and communicates this information to interested parties.

1.description: Identify, document and communicate the critical external services on which the entity depends, respecting the need to know principle.
indicators: List all external services and suppliers that the company depends on to function (internet, email, web hosting, cloud software, accounting, etc.).
Identify which of these services are critical (the failure of which would impede the normal functioning of the company).
Document the list with the supplier's name, emergency contact and criticality level.
Communicate this list to relevant internal stakeholders, sharing only what is necessary.
evidence: Excel sheet or PDF document with columns: External service | Supplier | Emergency contact | Rating (critical/non-critical) | Identification date, signed by the IT manager.
Email or meeting minutes proving communication of this list to internal interested parties.
1.tip: Think about all the services you use every day and ask yourself: if this supplier stopped tomorrow, would you be able to work? If not, it is a critical dependency.
1.effort: 2–4 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Classify, prioritize and integrate external critical services into the risk management process, including classification and prioritization criteria.
indicators: Define criteria for classifying and prioritizing external critical services (e.g. impact on operations, maximum tolerable time of unavailability, possibility of replacement).
Apply the criteria to each critical external service and assign priorities.
Integrate these services into the risk management process, ensuring they are included in risk assessments.
Register and approve the result of classification and integration.
evidence: PDF or Excel document with columns: External service | Classification criteria | Priority | Integrated in risk management (yes/no), approved by management.
Print or excerpt of the risk management register showing the inclusion of critical external services.
2.tip: When prioritizing, also consider whether there is an alternative supplier available in the market. Dependencies with no alternative are always higher priority.
2.effort: 3–5 hours | One person | No additional cost

GR.GR-1.title: The entity's risk appetite and tolerance are defined, approved, communicated and reviewed regularly
GR.GR-1.description: The company defines how much risk it is willing to accept in terms of cybersecurity. This helps you make more informed decisions about where to invest protection effort and resources.

2.description: Define, record, approve, regularly review and internally communicate the entity's risk appetite and tolerance.
indicators: Write a risk appetite and tolerance statement, explaining the level of risk the company is willing to accept (e.g. 'we accept low risks, but we do not tolerate risks that could stop the business for more than 4 hours').
Submit the declaration for formal management approval.
Communicate the approved statement to all relevant personnel.
Define a review periodicity (e.g. annually or whenever there is a significant change in the business).
Record each review carried out, even if it does not result in changes.
evidence: PDF document with the risk appetite and tolerance statement, with sections: Definition of risk appetite | Tolerance limits | Approval date | Next review, signed by management.
Communication record (email or meeting minutes) proving that relevant personnel were aware.
2.tip: Risk appetite is simply management's answer to the question: How much risk are we willing to take? Use simple language and give concrete examples of the company's day-to-day activities.
2.effort: 3–5 hours | One person (with direction) | No additional cost

GR.GR-2.title: Cybersecurity risk management processes are included in the entity's transversal risk management strategy
GR.GR-2.description: The company guarantees that cybersecurity risks are treated in the same way as other business risks (financial, operational, legal), and that management monitors them regularly.

1.description: Identify, approve, manage and keep cybersecurity risk management processes updated.
indicators: Define the phases of the cybersecurity risk management process (identification, analysis, assessment, treatment, monitoring).
Document the process and submit it for management approval.
Run the process at least once, recording the results of each phase.
Define a periodicity for updating the process (e.g. annual).
evidence: PDF document describing the cybersecurity risk management process with sections: Phases | Responsible | Periodicity | Approval date, approved by management.
Record (Excel or PDF) of the results of the process execution: risks identified, analyzed, evaluated and treated.
1.tip: It doesn't need a complex process. A simple cycle of 'identify risks, assess, decide what to do, record' already fulfills the essentials for an SME.
1.effort: 6–10 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Integrate cybersecurity risk management processes into the entity's transversal risk management strategy.
indicators: Identify the company's overall risk management strategy or process (if it exists).
Include cybersecurity risks in the same register or risk table as the company's other risks.
Ensure that management analyzes cybersecurity risks alongside other risks at regular management meetings.
Record this integration and management’s participation in the joint analysis.
evidence: Global risk register of the company (Excel or system) which lists cybersecurity risks side by side with other risks (financial, operational, etc.).
Minutes of a board or management meeting where cybersecurity risks were analyzed together with other risks.
2.tip: If the company already has a financial or operational risk sheet, simply add a column or tab for cybersecurity risks. Do not create a separate process.
2.effort: 3–5 hours | One person | No additional cost

GR.GR-3.title: The entity defines its risk treatment strategy
GR.GR-3.description: The company decides, for each identified risk, what it will do: accept it, reduce it, transfer it (e.g. insurance) or eliminate it. This decision is communicated to interested parties.

2.description: Define and communicate to interested parties the entity's risk treatment strategy.
indicators: Define which risk treatment options the company adopts (accept, reduce, transfer or eliminate).
For each risk identified in the risk register, assign the chosen treatment option and the respective person responsible.
Document the risk treatment strategy in a formal document and submit it for management approval.
Communicate the strategy to interested parties (IT staff, management, relevant business areas).
evidence: PDF document with the risk treatment strategy, including sections: Treatment options | Decision criteria | Approval date, signed by management.
Excel sheet or risk register with additional columns: Risk | Treatment option | Responsible | State.
Email or minutes proving communication of the strategy to interested parties.
2.tip: The four treatment options are: accept (live with the risk), reduce (apply measures), transfer (take out insurance or outsource) and eliminate (stop the activity that generates the risk).
2.effort: 4–6 hours | One person (with direction) | No additional cost

GR.GR-4.title: The entity establishes an internal communication channel to share cybersecurity risks
GR.GR-4.description: The company creates a way to communicate cybersecurity risks internally, so that management and relevant areas are always informed.

2.description: Establish an internal communication channel to share information about cybersecurity risks with management and relevant areas.
indicators: Choose the internal channel to use to communicate cybersecurity risks (e.g. dedicated email, Teams/Slack channel, periodic meeting, shared folder).
Define who can submit information about risks and who should receive it (management, IT manager, business areas).
Document the chosen channel, the rules of use and the participants.
Publicize the channel to relevant personnel and test its operation with a test communication.
evidence: PDF document or email with the definition of the communication channel: Chosen channel | Who sends | Who receives | Periodicity | Rules for use, approved by management.
Screen print of the channel in operation (e.g. Teams channel, dedicated email box, shared folder with records).
2.tip: In a small company, it could be as simple as a monthly email from the IT manager to management summarizing current risks. The important thing is that it exists and is regular.
2.effort: 1–2 hours | One person | No additional cost

GR.GR-5.title: The entity defines a Cybersecurity Risk Management Policy which provides a methodology for managing cybersecurity risks
GR.GR-5.description: The company creates a policy that describes how it will manage cybersecurity risks, including a method for calculating, recording, classifying and prioritizing them.

2.description: Define a methodology for managing cybersecurity risks that allows identifying, recording, calculating, classifying and prioritizing risks.
indicators: Choose or adapt a risk management methodology (e.g. based on probability x impact, with a scale of 1 to 5).
Document the methodology, including: how to identify risks, how to calculate the risk level, classification criteria and prioritization rules.
Include the methodology in the Cybersecurity Risk Management Policy.
Submit the policy for management approval.
Apply the methodology to the existing risk register to validate its operation.
evidence: Cybersecurity Risk Management Policy in PDF with section dedicated to methodology, including: Identification method | Risk calculation formula or table | Classification criteria | Prioritization rules | Approval date, signed by management.
Example of applying the methodology to the risk register (e.g. Excel sheet with one or two risks calculated and classified according to the methodology).
2.tip: A simple table of probability (1-5) times impact (1-5) is sufficient for most SMEs. The result gives you risk level from 1 to 25, easy to prioritize.
2.effort: 6–10 hours | One person | No additional cost

GR.FR-1.title: Management, direction and administration bodies understand their roles, responsibilities and promote a culture of cybersecurity, ethics and continuous improvement
GR.FR-1.description: The company's management and administration demonstrate that they understand their role in cybersecurity and provide the necessary resources to continuously improve it.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Management and administration must provide the necessary resources for the continuous improvement of cybersecurity, through an approved plan with measures, deadlines and resources.
indicators: Develop a Continuous Cybersecurity Improvement Plan, with concrete measures to implement (e.g. annual training, updating tools, reviewing processes).
For each measure, define realistic deadlines and the necessary resources (human, financial, technological).
Submit the plan for approval by management or administration.
Schedule the measures and assign those responsible for their execution.
Review the plan periodically to track progress and adjust as needed.
evidence: Cybersecurity Continuous Improvement Plan in PDF with columns: Measure | Deadline | Resources to be affected | Responsible | State, approved and signed by management/administration.
Record of review of the plan (minutes or updated document), demonstrating monitoring by management.
3.tip: The plan does not need to be ambitious. Three to five improvements per year, with defined deadlines and responsible people, already show real commitment from management.
3.effort: 4–8 hours | One person (with direction) | No additional cost

GR.FR-2.title: Adequate and proportionate resources are allocated to the entity's cybersecurity risk management strategy, roles, responsibilities and cybersecurity policies
GR.FR-2.description: The company guarantees that it allocates proportionate people, time and budget to the management of cybersecurity risks, in accordance with the defined functions and policies.

2.description: Allocate sufficient and proportionate resources to cybersecurity risk management, with short- or medium-term activity planning.
indicators: List the cybersecurity activities planned for the next 6 to 12 months (e.g. training, updates, acquisition of tools, audits).
For each activity, estimate the necessary resources (staff hours, budget, tools).
Submit the planning for management approval, ensuring that there is budgetary or personnel allocation.
Record the effective allocation of resources throughout the period.
evidence: Cybersecurity activity plan in PDF or Excel with columns: Activity | Deadline | Required resources (hours/budget) | Responsible | State, approved by management.
Record or evidence of resource allocation (e.g. training invoice, hours logged, proof of tool purchase).
2.tip: Including cybersecurity in the annual budget, even if it is a small amount, is the most important gesture. It shows that management takes the issue seriously.
2.effort: 3–5 hours | One person (with direction) | No additional cost

GR.FR-3.title: Cybersecurity roles, responsibilities and authorities are defined and communicated to staff, suppliers and stakeholders
GR.FR-3.description: The company documents who does what in cybersecurity (who is responsible for what, who has the authority to decide) and communicates this information to everyone involved.

2.description: Document, approve, communicate and keep cybersecurity roles, responsibilities and authorities updated, aligned with internal functions and external entities.
indicators: Identify all cybersecurity roles in the company (e.g. IT manager, cybersecurity manager, management, users).
For each role, define concrete responsibilities and authorities (what can decide, approve or execute).
Document everything in a formal document (e.g. RACI Matrix or table of roles and responsibilities).
Submit for management approval.
Communicate the document to staff, suppliers and relevant stakeholders.
Define an update rule (e.g. review whenever there are organizational changes).
evidence: PDF document with table of cybersecurity roles, responsibilities and authorities, with columns: Role | Responsibilities | Authorities | Holder, approved and signed by management.
Email or minutes proving communication to interested parties (internal and external).
2.tip: In an SME with 20 people you don't need a complex matrix. A simple table with 4-5 lines (management, IT manager, users, suppliers) already covers the essentials.
2.effort: 3–5 hours | One person | No additional cost

GR.FR-4.title: Cybersecurity is included in human resources management processes
GR.FR-4.description: The company includes cybersecurity considerations in personnel management processes: from hiring, during the employment relationship and until the employee leaves.

1.description: Define an Acceptable Use Policy, make it available to staff at the entrance and ensure awareness of compliance.
indicators: Write an Acceptable Use Policy that defines the rules for using the company's technological resources (computers, email, internet, cell phones, etc.).
Submit the policy for management approval.
Deliver the policy to each employee upon joining the company.
Obtain a signed declaration of acknowledgment from each employee.
Make the policy available in a location accessible to everyone (intranet, shared folder).
evidence: Acceptable Use Policy in PDF, approved by management, with sections: Rules for using equipment | Email and internet | Passwords | Personal devices | Approval date.
Excel sheet or record with columns: Employee name | Policy delivery date | Signature/declaration of acknowledgment.
1.tip: The Acceptable Use Policy is the 'digital good behavior contract'. It should be short (2-3 pages), written in simple language and delivered on the first day of work.
1.effort: 4–6 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Develop and maintain cybersecurity measures associated with human resources management, from recruitment to staff departure.
indicators: Define the cybersecurity measures to be applied at each stage of the employee's life cycle: recruitment, entry, during the contract and exit.
At the entrance: access allocation forecast, delivery of configured equipment, signing of the Acceptable Use Policy.
During the contract: periodic review of access, cybersecurity training, compliance monitoring.
Upon exit: immediate revocation of access, return of equipment, deletion of accounts.
Document these measures and ensure that HR and IT managers are aware of them.
evidence: PDF document with cybersecurity measures for HR, organized by phases: Recruitment | Entrance | During contract | Output, with concrete actions for each phase, signed by management or HR.
Completed checklist (PDF or Excel) of at least one example of employee entry and/or exit, with evidence of compliance with the defined measures.
2.tip: The most critical step is leaving: when someone leaves the company, their access must be cut off on the same day. Create an exit checklist so you never forget.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | One person (IT + HR) | No additional cost

GR.PP-1.title: Policies for Cybersecurity management, including the Cybersecurity Policy, are defined based on the organizational context, the cybersecurity strategy and the entity's priorities
GR.PP-1.description: The company creates its main Cybersecurity Policy and identifies the various sub-policies that make up cybersecurity management (e.g.: risk management, passwords, backups, access).

1.description: Define, document, approve and internally disclose the Cybersecurity Policy, with identification and assignment of roles and responsibilities.
indicators: Write the company's Cybersecurity Policy, including: objective, scope, general principles, identification of applicable sub-policies and assignment of roles and responsibilities.
Submit the policy for approval by management, management or administration bodies.
Disclose the policy internally to all staff (by email, intranet or meeting).
Record the communication (date, medium used and recipients).
evidence: Cybersecurity Policy in PDF with sections: Objective | Scope | Principles | Identified sub-policies | Roles and responsibilities | Approval date, signed by management/administration.
Email or meeting minutes proving the disclosure of the policy to staff, with date and list of recipients.
1.tip: The Cybersecurity Policy is the 'mother' document. You don't need to detail everything, but you should reference the other policies (passwords, access, backups) that complement it.
1.effort: 6–10 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Define, approve and implement a Data Classification and Management Policy.
indicators: Write the Data Classification and Management Policy, defining: classification categories (e.g. public, internal, confidential, restricted), treatment rules for each category and those responsible.
Include guidance on storing, sharing and destroying data according to its classification.
Submit the policy for management approval.
Communicate the policy to staff and ensure its practical application on a daily basis.
evidence: Classification and Data Management Policy in PDF with sections: Classification categories | Treatment rules by category | Storage | Share | Destruction | Responsible, approved by management.
Record of communication of the policy to staff (email or minutes).
2.tip: Start with 3 simple categories: Public (can be seen by anyone), Internal (company personnel only) and Confidential (restricted access). Don't complicate things with more categories.
2.effort: 6–10 hours | One person | No additional cost

GR.PP-2.title: The Incident Response Plan is defined, communicated, maintained and improved
GR.PP-2.description: The company creates and maintains a plan that defines what to do when a cybersecurity incident occurs: who does what, how to communicate, and what steps to take.

1.description: Define, maintain and approve an Incident Response Plan with identification and assignment of roles and responsibilities.
indicators: Write the Incident Response Plan, including: types of anticipated incidents, response steps for each type, who is responsible for each action and emergency contacts.
Include in the plan the identification of roles and responsibilities (who detects, who decides, who communicates, who resolves).
Submit the plan for management approval.
Communicate the plan to relevant staff and ensure it is accessible in the event of an emergency.
Define a periodicity for reviewing the plan (e.g. annually or after each incident).
evidence: Incident Response Plan in PDF with sections: Types of incidents | Response steps | Roles and responsibilities | Emergency contacts | Approval date, approved by management.
Record of communication of the plan to staff (email or meeting minutes).
1.tip: The plan should fit into 3-4 pages and answer three questions: What happened? Who notice? What do I do next? Hang an abridged version next to computers.
1.effort: 6–10 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Substantial

GR.PP-3.title: The Disaster Recovery Plan is defined, communicated, maintained and improved
GR.PP-3.description: The company creates and maintains a plan that describes how to recover its systems and data after a serious disaster (e.g., destructive computer attack, fire, flood).

2.description: Define, approve and implement the Disaster Recovery Plan with roles and responsibilities. Integrate cybersecurity requirements into supplier contracts.
indicators: Write the Disaster Recovery Plan, including: considered disaster scenarios, recovery procedures for each critical system, recovery priority order, roles and responsibilities.
Submit the plan for management approval.
Communicate the plan to key personnel and relevant suppliers.
Include cybersecurity clauses in contracts with suppliers (e.g. response times, incident notification obligations, cooperation in the event of a disaster).
Test the plan at least once to validate its effectiveness.
evidence: Disaster Recovery Plan in PDF with sections: Scenarios | Recovery Procedures | Priority | Roles and responsibilities | Contacts, approved by management.
Template contract or contractual clause with cybersecurity requirements (PDF), including: response times | notification obligations | disaster cooperation.
2.tip: Test the plan with a simple exercise: simulate that the main server crashes and follow the steps in the plan to check that everything works. Write down what failed to correct.
2.effort: 10–16 hours | One to two people | No additional cost

GR.SP-1.title: The results of the cybersecurity risk management strategy are analyzed to inform management, management and administration bodies and, if necessary, adapt the strategy
GR.SP-1.description: Those responsible for cybersecurity analyze the results of the risk management strategy and inform management. If necessary, the strategy is adjusted taking into account operational limitations, budget or changes in the business.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: Analyze the results of the cybersecurity risk management strategy, report to management and adapt the strategy as necessary.
indicators: Define a periodicity for analyzing the results of the cybersecurity risk management strategy (e.g. quarterly or semi-annual).
Prepare a summary report with the main indicators: risks addressed, pending risks, incidents that occurred, effectiveness of the measures implemented.
Present the report to management or administration, indicating whether the strategy needs adjustments.
Record the decisions made and adjustments approved by management.
evidence: Periodic report in PDF with sections: Risk summary | Measures implemented | Effectiveness | Fit Recommendations | Date, presented to management.
Minutes of the meeting with management, recording the analysis of the results and the decisions made on the strategy.
4.tip: A half-page report with 3 points (what went well, what went wrong, what to adjust) is enough to keep management informed without overloading it.
4.effort: 2–4 hours per report | One person | No additional cost

GR.SP-2.title: The cybersecurity risk management strategy is regularly reviewed and adapted to ensure coverage of organizational requirements and risks
GR.SP-2.description: The company regularly reviews its cybersecurity risk management strategy, incorporating lessons from audits, incidents and the performance of cybersecurity functions.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Regularly review the cybersecurity risk management strategy and adapt it based on the updated threat landscape and organizational requirements.
indicators: Define a calendar for reviewing the cybersecurity risk management strategy (e.g. biannual or annual).
Before each review, update the threat landscape (known risks, new vulnerabilities, recent incidents in the sector).
Compare current strategy with updated threats and organizational requirements.
Propose and approve the necessary adjustments to the strategy.
Record changes made and communicate updates to relevant staff.
evidence: Strategy review log in PDF with sections: Review date | Updated threat landscape | Proposed changes | Changes approved, signed by management.
Updated version of the risk management strategy with registration of changes (version control).
3.tip: Subscribe to CNCS and ENISA alerts to stay up to date with new threats. Use this information to feed into the strategy review.
3.effort: 4–8 hours per review | One person | No additional cost

GR.SP-3.title: The entity's cybersecurity risk management performance is assessed and reviewed for necessary changes
GR.SP-3.description: The company measures and evaluates whether its cybersecurity risk management is working well, using performance indicators, and makes changes when necessary.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Define and implement performance assessment mechanisms for cybersecurity risk management.
indicators: Define performance indicators (KPIs) for cybersecurity risk management (e.g. number of risks treated vs. pending, average resolution time, percentage of controls implemented).
Choose the tool or method for collecting indicators (e.g. Excel sheet, simple control panel).
Collect the indicators with the defined periodicity.
Analyze results and propose improvements when indicators do not meet objectives.
Record the analysis and decisions made.
evidence: PDF or Excel document with the definition of performance indicators: Indicator | Calculation formula | Objective | Collection frequency | Responsible.
Record of the results of the indicators collected, with analysis and decisions made, signed by the person responsible for cybersecurity.
3.tip: Start with 2-3 simple indicators (e.g. percentage of risks handled, number of incidents in the quarter). Don't try to measure everything from the beginning.
3.effort: 4–6 hours (setup) + 1–2 hours per collection | One person | No additional cost

GR.CA-1.title: Supply chain risk management policies and processes are identified, established, evaluated and managed
GR.CA-1.description: The company defines rules and processes to manage security risks linked to its suppliers and technology partners, including cloud services.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Define, approve, implement, review and update the Supply Chain Risk Management Policy and the respective procedure.
indicators: Write the Supply Chain Risk Management Policy, including: scope, supplier evaluation criteria, security requirements and monitoring process.
Define the associated operational procedure (how to evaluate a supplier, what questions to ask, how to record the results).
Submit the policy and procedure for management approval.
Implement the policy, applying it to current suppliers.
Define a review and update periodicity (e.g. annual).
evidence: Supply Chain Risk Management Policy in PDF with sections: Scope | Assessment criteria | Security requirements | Monitoring process | Approval date, signed by management.
Operational procedure in PDF describing the supplier evaluation steps, approved and dated.
3.tip: Start with the vendors who have access to your systems or data. These are the ones with the highest risk and should be evaluated first.
3.effort: 10–16 hours | One person | No additional cost

GR.CA-2.title: The roles and responsibilities of partners, suppliers and customers within the scope of cybersecurity management are defined, communicated and coordinated internally and externally
GR.CA-2.description: The company clearly defines who is responsible for what in terms of cybersecurity in its relationship with partners, suppliers and customers, and communicates these responsibilities to all parties.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: Define, coordinate and communicate the cybersecurity roles and responsibilities of external stakeholders (partners, suppliers, customers), carrying out awareness raising actions.
indicators: Identify external stakeholders relevant to the company's cybersecurity (suppliers, partners, key customers).
For each external stakeholder, define cybersecurity roles and responsibilities (what they should do, what they should ensure).
Formally communicate these responsibilities to external parties (e.g. by letter, email or through contractual clauses).
Carry out awareness raising actions to ensure that external parties understand what is expected of them.
evidence: PDF or Excel document with columns: External stakeholder | Cybersecurity Role/Responsibility | Form of communication | Date of communication, signed by the person responsible for cybersecurity or management.
Email, letter or contract excerpt proving communication of responsibilities to external parties.
4.tip: Include a cybersecurity section in contracts with suppliers, even if simple. Something like: 'The supplier undertakes to report any security incident that may affect company data within 24 hours.'
4.effort: 4–8 hours | One person | No additional cost

GR.CA-3.title: The entity's supply chain cybersecurity risk management processes are included in the entity's cross-cutting cybersecurity processes and risk management strategy
GR.CA-3.description: The company integrates the risks of its suppliers and partners into global cybersecurity risk management, ensuring that they are monitored and treated on an ongoing basis.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Identify, approve, manage and keep supply chain cybersecurity risks updated, integrating them into transversal processes. Include clauses in contracts that allow for continuous evaluation of suppliers.
indicators: Identify and record cybersecurity risks specific to the supply chain (e.g. supplier with VPN access, cloud service with sensitive data).
Integrate these risks into the company's global cybersecurity risk register.
Include clauses in contracts with suppliers that allow you to assess the supplier's cybersecurity and correct identified flaws.
Submit the identification and integration of risks for management approval.
Update risks whenever there are changes in the supply chain (new suppliers, termination of contracts).
evidence: Risk register (Excel or PDF) that includes a section or tab dedicated to supply chain risks, with columns: Supplier | Risk identified | Risk level | Treatment | Date, approved by management.
PDF contract or contractual clause template with provisions for: continuous assessment of supplier cybersecurity | correction of identified flaws.
3.tip: When renewing contracts with suppliers, take the opportunity to include cybersecurity clauses. It is simpler and more natural than trying to renegotiate existing contracts.
3.effort: 6–10 hours | One person | No additional cost

GR.CA-4.title: Suppliers are periodically evaluated
GR.CA-4.description: The company regularly evaluates its suppliers to check whether they meet the required cybersecurity requirements, in accordance with the services they provide.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: Define and apply a periodic evaluation process for suppliers in accordance with contracted services.
indicators: Define a periodicity for evaluating suppliers (e.g. annually or when there are contractual changes).
Prepare a questionnaire or assessment checklist with the most relevant cybersecurity requirements for the service provided.
Apply the questionnaire or checklist to suppliers, recording the results.
Analyze the results and, if necessary, communicate the required improvements to the supplier.
Record the assessment and compliance status of each supplier.
evidence: Supplier assessment questionnaire or checklist in PDF or Excel, with fields: Security requirement | According (yes/no) | Observations.
Excel sheet with columns: Supplier | Valuation date | Overall result | Corrective actions requested | State, signed by the IT manager.
4.tip: Start with a simple 10-question questionnaire about the vendor's security practices (backups, updates, access management). It doesn't need to be a full audit.
4.effort: 2–4 hours per vendor | One person | No additional cost

GR.CA-5.title: Entities define and integrate cybersecurity risk management requirements for the supply chain into supplier contracts and other agreements
GR.CA-5.description: The company includes clear cybersecurity requirements in its supplier contracts to ensure that suppliers comply with the required security standards.

2.description: Define and integrate cybersecurity requirements and means for sharing sensitive information into contracts and other supplier agreements.
indicators: Draft a contract template or contractual addendum with specific cybersecurity clauses (e.g., confidentiality obligations, incident notification, authentication requirements, data protection, conditions for subcontracting).
Define the minimum security requirements for each supplier, tailored to the type of access they have to the entity's systems or data.
Include conditions in the contracts for the secure sharing of sensitive information between the entity and the supplier.
Apply the updated template to all new contracts and, progressively, to contracts undergoing renewal.
Maintain a registry of contracts that already include the required cybersecurity clauses.
evidence: PDF or Word document containing the contract template or addendum, including the cybersecurity clauses, with the approval date and signature of the legal or IT manager.
Excel spreadsheet with the columns: Supplier | Type of access | Updated contract (Yes/No) | Signature date | Person in charge, signed by the IT manager.
2.tip: It is not necessary to renegotiate all contracts immediately. Start with suppliers who access your systems or process sensitive data, and apply the new clauses to contracts that are up for renewal or negotiation.
2.effort: 8–16 hours | One to two people (IT and Legal) | No additional cost

3.description: Integrate provisions into contracts that allow for continuous assessment of suppliers' cybersecurity and the remediation of any identified vulnerabilities.
indicators: Include clauses in contracts that allow the entity to conduct or request security assessments from the supplier (e.g., right to audit, access to security reports, shared security tests).
Define in the contracts the supplier's obligation to periodically report security metrics or incidents that have occurred.
Establish specific deadlines and procedures in the contracts for the supplier to remediate identified security vulnerabilities.
Include penalties or termination clauses in the contracts in case of repeated non-compliance with cybersecurity requirements.
Update the contracts of the most critical suppliers to include these provisions, on a defined periodic basis.
evidence: PDF or Word document with the updated contract template, containing right-to-audit clauses, remediation deadlines, and penalties, with the approval date and signature of the legal manager.

Excel spreadsheet or PDF with a record of assessments conducted on suppliers under contractual clauses, with the columns: Supplier | Assessment date | Identified vulnerabilities | Remediation deadline | Status, signed by the IT manager.
Copies of emails or letters sent to suppliers notifying them of identified vulnerabilities and following up on agreed remediations.
3.tip: The 'right to audit' does not require constant audits. It is enough to have the clause in the contract and use it when necessary, for example, after an incident or during an annual review. This is sufficient to meet this level.
3.effort: 16–24 hours | Two people (IT and Legal) | Variable cost depending on the need for external legal support

GR.CA-6.title: Before hiring suppliers or third parties, due diligence is carried out to reduce risks
GR.CA-6.description: The company carries out a security check before hiring a new supplier, to ensure that it is not exposing itself to unnecessary risks.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Before formalizing contractual relationships, conduct due diligence processes to minimize risk exposure.
indicators: Define a due diligence process to be applied before hiring suppliers (e.g. security questionnaire, certification verification, reputation analysis).
Apply the process to each new supplier before signing the contract.
Record the results of due diligence and the decision made (approve, reject, approve with conditions).
File due diligence documentation with the hiring process.
evidence: PDF document describing the due diligence process: Steps | Assessment criteria | Responsible.

Due diligence record completed for each supplier evaluated (PDF or Excel), with columns: Supplier | Date | Criteria evaluated | Result | Decision, signed by the person responsible for IT or purchasing.
3.tip: Due diligence can be as simple as sending a 10-question security questionnaire to the supplier before signing the contract. The important thing is to do it before and not after.
3.effort: 2–3 hours per vendor | One person | No additional cost

GR.CA-7.title: The entity assesses supply chain cybersecurity risks
GR.CA-7.description: The company regularly assesses the cybersecurity risks associated with its suppliers, classifying them according to the exposure of information, the impact on the chain and the type of services provided.

1.description: Carry out supply chain risk assessments at least annually or whenever there are relevant changes.
indicators: List all suppliers in the supply chain that have an impact on the company's cybersecurity.
For each supplier, identify the associated cybersecurity risks (e.g. data access, operational dependency, incident history).
Record the risk assessment in a document with a date and person responsible.
Repeat the assessment at least once a year or when there are significant changes (new supplier, new service, incident).
evidence: Excel sheet with columns: Supplier | Service provided | Risks identified | Risk level | Assessment date | Responsible, signed by the IT manager.
Record of date and signature of the last supply chain risk assessment.
1.tip: Group suppliers by type of access: those who access your systems, those who process your data and those who provide services without access. The first ones are the ones with the highest risk.
1.effort: 4–8 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Mitigate risks identified during the supply chain risk assessment process.
indicators: For each risk identified in the supply chain assessment, define the appropriate mitigation measure (e.g. require multi-factor authentication from the supplier, limit access, include contractual clauses).
Implement the defined mitigation measures.
Record the implementation status of each measure.
Communicate to suppliers the measures required of them.
evidence: Excel sheet with columns: Risk identified | Mitigation measure | Implementation date | Status | Responsible, signed by the IT manager.
Communications (emails or letters) sent to suppliers regarding the required measures.
2.tip: Start with the simplest and most impactful measures: limiting supplier access to the minimum necessary and requiring them to use multi-factor authentication.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | One person | Variable cost (according to measurements)

3.description: Carry out cybersecurity assessments of suppliers in accordance with the required level of demand (certifications, audits, tests).
indicators: Define the level of cybersecurity requirements for each critical supplier (e.g. certification, external audit, security testing).
Request critical suppliers to provide evidence of compliance (certificates, audit reports, security test results).
Analyze the documentation received and check whether it meets the defined level of requirements.
Record the assessment and level of compliance of each supplier.
Define actions for suppliers that do not meet requirements.
evidence: Evaluation record for each supplier in PDF or Excel with columns: Supplier | Requirement level | Evidence presented | Compliance | Pending actions, signed by the IT manager.
Copies of certificates, audit reports or test results submitted by suppliers.
3.tip: If the supplier has the Digital Maturity Seal or an ISO 27001 certification, this is good evidence. For others, ask for at least a recent audit report.
3.effort: 3–6 hours per vendor | One person | No additional cost (supplier costs)

GR.CA-8.title: The Incident Response Plan and Disaster Recovery Plan are tested with monitoring from suppliers
GR.CA-8.description: The company identifies and involves key suppliers in testing Incident Response and Disaster Recovery Plans to ensure everyone knows what to do in an emergency.

2.description: Identify key supplier personnel to be included in the activities foreseen in the Incident Response and Disaster Recovery Plans.
indicators: Identify which suppliers have a relevant role in the event of an incident or disaster (e.g. internet provider, cloud hosting, technical support).
For each relevant supplier, identify the key contact (name, telephone, email) to be involved in the plans.
Include these contacts in Incident Response and Disaster Recovery Plans.
Communicate to suppliers who are included in the plans and what their responsibilities are.
evidence: List of key supplier contacts included in Incident Response and Disaster Recovery Plans (PDF), with columns: Supplier | Key contact | Telephone | Email | Responsibility.
Email or letter sent to suppliers communicating their inclusion in the plans and respective responsibilities.
2.tip: Always have two contacts per supplier (main and alternate). In a real incident, you cannot depend on a single person who may be unavailable.
2.effort: 2–4 hours | One person | No additional cost

3.description: Include key supplier personnel in Incident Response and Disaster Recovery Plan exercises and testing.
indicators: Plan exercises or testing of plans that include participation from key suppliers.
Invite key supplier contacts to participate in the exercises (by email or formal letter).
Carry out the exercise, recording the participation of suppliers and the results obtained.
Analyze results and identify areas for improvement, including coordination with suppliers.
Update plans with lessons learned.
evidence: Exercise record in PDF with sections: Date | Tested scenario | Participants (internal and suppliers) | Results | Lessons learned | Improvements to be implemented, signed by the IT manager.
Invitations sent to suppliers (emails) and confirmation of participation.
3.tip: Start with tabletop exercises (simulation in a meeting, without touching the systems). They are simpler to organize and allow you to test coordination with suppliers.
3.effort: 4–8 hours per exercise | One to two people | No additional cost

GR.CA-9.title: Supply chain cybersecurity practices are integrated into cybersecurity and organizational risk management programs, and their performance is monitored throughout the lifecycle of technological products and services
GR.CA-9.description: The company guarantees that security practices related to suppliers are included in the Cybersecurity Policy and that it continuously monitors the security of all technological products and services it acquires throughout their life cycle.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: Integrate supply chain cybersecurity practices into the Cybersecurity Policy and continuously monitor the life cycle of purchased technological products and services.
indicators: Include a section dedicated to supply chain security in the Cybersecurity Policy.
Define monitoring practices to be applied throughout the life cycle of technological products and services (from acquisition to deactivation).
Record the monitoring activities carried out (e.g. checking for updates, reviewing supplier permissions, periodic evaluation).
Periodically review practices and update as necessary.
evidence: Excerpt from the Cybersecurity Policy (PDF) with the section dedicated to the supply chain.
Monitoring record in Excel or PDF with columns: Product/Service | Supplier | Life cycle phase | Monitoring activity | Date | Result | Responsible.
4.tip: Create a quarterly reminder to check whether vendors have submitted security updates and whether the access permissions they were given are still required.
4.effort: 4–6 hours (setup) + 2 hours per review | One person | No additional cost

GR.CA-10.title: When defining cybersecurity supply chain risk management processes, the entity includes provisions for activities that may occur during or after the termination of contracts with suppliers or partners
GR.CA-10.description: The company has defined rules for when a contract with a supplier ends: how to cut access, recover data, revoke permissions and protect information shared during the contract.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: Define internal procedures for terminating relationships with suppliers, protecting the company from the risks inherent to contractual termination (unauthorized access, misuse of information).
indicators: Define a procedure for terminating contracts with suppliers, including: revocation of access, return or destruction of data, transfer of services.
Include this procedure in supply chain risk management documentation.
Apply the procedure whenever a contract with a supplier ends.
Record the execution of the procedure for each contractual termination.
evidence: PDF document with the contractual termination procedure, including sections: Revocation of access | Return/destruction of data | Transfer of services | Responsible | Deadlines.
Completed checklist (PDF or Excel) for each contractual termination executed, with columns: Supplier | Termination date | Access revoked (yes/no) | Data returned/destroyed (yes/no) | Responsible.
4.tip: Create a 'supplier exit' checklist similar to the employee exit checklist: cut access, collect credentials, confirm data destruction, on the day of termination.
4.effort: 3–5 hours | One person | No additional cost

ID.GA-1.title: The equipment and other physical resources existing in the entity are inventoried
ID.GA-1.description: The company maintains an updated list of all the equipment and physical resources it has (computers, servers, printers, routers, cell phones, etc.), so it knows exactly what it has and where it is.

1.description: Document and maintain the entity's inventory of equipment and physical resources.
indicators: List all the company's equipment and physical resources (computers, laptops, servers, printers, routers, switches, cell phones, tablets, external hard drives, etc.).
For each piece of equipment, record: type, brand, model, serial number, location, person responsible and status (active, in use, in storage, scrapped).
Save the inventory in a centralized file (Excel or asset management system).
Define a frequency for updating the inventory (e.g. quarterly or whenever there are changes).
evidence: Excel sheet or inventory record with columns: Type | Brand | Model | Serial number | Location | Responsible | Status | Date of last update, validated by the IT manager.
Record of the last inventory update date.
1.tip: Start by going through all the offices with an Excel sheet and photographing the equipment. Use the manufacturer's label to quickly note the make, model and serial number.
1.effort: 4–8 hours (depending on size) | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Use tools to support the management and inventory of equipment and physical resources.
indicators: Select and implement a physical asset management tool (e.g. GLPI, Snipe-IT, Lansweeper, or another suitable for the company's size).
Migrate existing inventory (from Excel) to the tool.
Configure alerts or notifications for equipment nearing end of life or warranty.
Train IT staff in using the tool.
Periodically validate that the tool reflects physical reality.
evidence: Screenshot of the inventory tool showing the list of equipment with the fields: Type | Brand | Model | Serial number | Location | Responsible | State.
Registration of the tool's automatic alert or report configuration.
3.tip: GLPI and Snipe-IT are free and open source tools, ideal for SMEs that want to take the next step without licensing costs.
3.effort: 8–16 hours (installation and migration) | An IT person | Cost: free (open-source tools) or variable (commercial tools)

ID.GA-2.title: The logical resources that support the entity's service processes are inventoried
ID.GA-2.description: The company maintains an updated list of all the software, applications and platforms it uses to operate (billing system, email, ERP, antivirus, etc.), knowing who uses them and where they are installed.

1.description: Document and keep the inventory of logical resources (software, applications, platforms) used in the entity updated.
indicators: List all software and applications used in the company (operating systems, business applications, productivity tools, cloud services, antivirus, etc.).
For each logical resource, record: name, version, supplier, type of license, equipment where it is installed and responsible.
Store inventory in a centralized file.
Define an update frequency (e.g. quarterly or whenever there are installations/uninstallations).
evidence: Excel sheet or inventory record with columns: Software name | Version | Supplier | License type | Equipment where it is installed | Responsible | Date of last update, validated by the IT manager.
Record of the last software inventory update date.
1.tip: Use the Windows Control Panel (Programs and Features) or the 'Get-WmiObject Win32_Product' command in PowerShell to quickly get the list of software installed on each computer.
1.effort: 3–6 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Use tools or applications to support the management and inventory of logical resources.
indicators: Select and implement a software management tool (e.g. GLPI with inventory plugin, PDQ Inventory, Lansweeper).
Migrate existing software inventory to the tool.
Configure the tool to automatically detect software installed on network equipment.
Configure alerts for unauthorized software or expiring licenses.
Periodically validate that the tool reflects the software actually in use.
evidence: Screenshot of the software inventory tool showing the list of logical resources with the fields: Name | Version | Equipment | License | State.
Record of configured alerts (e.g. screenshot of alert configuration for unauthorized software).
3.tip: Many physical asset management tools (like GLPI) also do software inventory, saving you the need to have two separate tools.
3.effort: 6–12 hours (installation and configuration) | An IT person | Cost: free (open-source tools) or variable

ID.GA-3.title: Communication networks and flows are mapped
ID.GA-3.description: The company draws diagrams that show how its network is organized (computers, servers, routers, internet connections) and how information flows within and outside the organization.

2.description: Create network diagrams that demonstrate the topology and map internal communication flows between systems.
indicators: Draw a network diagram that shows all network equipment (routers, switches, firewalls, servers, Wi-Fi access points) and the connections between them.
Identify and map internal communication flows (which systems communicate with which other systems and for what purpose).
Include existing network segments in the diagram (e.g. internal network, guest network, DMZ if applicable).
Save the diagram in an accessible place and define someone responsible for updating it.
evidence: Network diagram in PDF or image with identification of: Network equipment | Servers | Network segments | Connections between components, with date and person responsible indicated.
Complementary document or diagram showing internal communication flows: System A communicates with System B for purpose X.
2.tip: Use draw.io (diagrams.net), which is free, to draw the network diagram. It doesn't need to be pixel perfect; it needs to be correct and understandable.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

3.description: Map external communication flows, document them, approve them and keep them updated.
indicators: Identify all external communication flows (internet connections, VPNs, remote access, connections to cloud services, communications with partners).
Document each external flow: origin, destination, purpose, protocol used and open ports.
Submit documentation of external flows for approval by management or IT manager.
Update diagrams whenever there are changes to external communications.
evidence: External communication flow diagram in PDF with identification of: Origin | Destination | Purpose | Protocol | Doors, approved and updated date.
Record of approval (signature or email) by management or IT manager.
3.tip: Start with the list of firewall rules. Most rules correspond to an external communication flow that can be documented directly.
3.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

ID.GA-4.title: Networks and external information systems are identified and cataloged
ID.GA-4.description: The company identifies and records the systems and networks it operates outside its facilities (e.g. cloud servers, externally hosted applications, equipment in third-party data centers), knowing where they are and who manages them.

2.description: Include in the asset inventory information systems that are located outside the entity's physical facilities.
indicators: Identify all of the company's information systems that are housed outside of its facilities (cloud servers, SaaS applications, external databases, equipment in third-party data centers).
For each external system, record: name, type, location (supplier/data center), internal responsible, external responsible (supplier) and criticality level.
Include this information in the existing asset inventory, in an integrated manner.
Update whenever there are changes (new services, migrations, closures).
evidence: Asset inventory (Excel or system) with tab or section dedicated to external systems, with columns: System name | Type | External location | Supplier | Internal responsible | Criticality | Update date, validated by the IT manager.
Record of the last update date of the external systems inventory.
2.tip: Make a quick list of all the cloud services you use (Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, invoicing software, etc.). There are often forgotten cloud services that are no longer used but are still active.
2.effort: 2–4 hours | One person | No additional cost

ID.GA-5.title: Assets are classified according to their criticality
ID.GA-5.description: The company classifies all its assets (equipment, software, data, people) according to their importance and value to the business, to know which ones deserve greater protection.

1.description: Classify assets (humans, hardware, software, devices, data, applications) according to their criticality and value to the entity.
indicators: Define a simple criticality rating scale (e.g. High, Medium, Low).
Define the criteria for each level (e.g. High = asset whose failure for the business; Medium = partial impact; Low = minimal impact).
Go through the inventory of assets (physical and logical) and assign a classification to each one.
Record the classification in the inventory, including the person responsible for the decision and the date.
evidence: Asset inventory (Excel or system) with additional column: Criticality (High/Medium/Low) | Criteria applied | Responsible for classification | Date, validated by the IT manager.
PDF document defining the classification criteria used.
1.tip: Ask for each asset: If this asset failed now, what would happen to the business? If the answer is 'I stopped everything', it's high. If it's 'workable', it's average. If it's 'hardly anyone noticed', it's low.
1.effort: 3–6 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Substantial

ID.GA-6.title: Corresponding data and metadata inventories for designated data types are maintained
ID.GA-6.description: The company maintains an up-to-date inventory of the data it processes (personal data, financial data, customer data, etc.), including where it is stored, who is responsible and how it is classified.

2.description: Identify and classify the data and respective metadata processed by the entity, in accordance with the Data Classification and Management Policy.
indicators: List the types of data that the company processes (personal data, financial data, commercial data, technical data, etc.).
For each type of data, record: where it is stored (system/server/cloud), who is responsible, who has access and what the classification is according to the Data Classification and Management Policy.
Register the associated metadata (format, retention period, legal basis if applicable).
Integrate this inventory into the global asset register or keep it as a complementary document.
Define a review frequency (e.g. annual).
evidence: Excel sheet with columns: Data type | Location | Responsible | Who has access | Classification (according to Policy) | Format | Retention period | Update date, signed by the IT manager or DPO.
Reference to the Classification and Data Management Policy used as a basis for classification.
2.tip: If the company has already carried out the GDPR exercise (registration of personal data processing activities), part of the work is done. Extend this record to other types of data.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | One person | No additional cost

ID.GA-7.title: Systems, hardware, software, services and data are managed throughout their life cycles
ID.GA-7.description: The company manages all its assets (equipment, software, services, data) from acquisition to destruction, ensuring that they are maintained, updated and protected at all stages.

1.description: Perform security updates regularly, apply corrections (patches) to applications and systems, and ensure periodic maintenance of equipment. Approve maintenance actions.
indicators: Define a frequency for system security updates (e.g. monthly or as soon as available).
Apply security patches and updates to all applications and systems, in order of criticality.
If an update cannot be applied, document the reason and apply compensatory control (e.g. isolate the system, limit access).
Ensure periodic maintenance of physical equipment (cleaning, condition check, replacement of worn components).
Register and approve each software and hardware maintenance action.
evidence: Record of applied security updates (Excel or system printout) with columns: System/Application | Patch/Update | Application date | Responsible.
Record of compensatory controls applied in case of impossibility of updating (PDF or Excel) with: System | Reason | Compensatory control | Date.
Equipment maintenance record (Excel) with columns: Equipment | Type of maintenance | Date | Approved by.
1.tip: Enable automatic updates whenever possible (Windows Update, antivirus updates). For software that doesn't automatically update, create a monthly reminder.
1.effort: 2–4 hours per month | An IT person | No additional cost

2.description: Define, approve and implement an Asset Lifecycle Management Policy. Plan and document preventive maintenance of critical components.
indicators: Write the Asset Lifecycle Management Policy, defining rules for: acquisition, use, maintenance, storage and destruction of assets.
Include measures to manage the loss, misuse, damage or theft of assets.
Submit the policy for management approval.
Create a preventive maintenance plan for critical system components (servers, network equipment, backup systems).
Perform and document preventive maintenance according to the plan.
evidence: Asset Lifecycle Management Policy in PDF with sections: Acquisition | Usage | Maintenance | Storage | Destruction | Loss/theft management, approved by management.
Preventive maintenance plan (Excel or PDF) with columns: Critical component | Type of maintenance | Periodicity | Last run | Next run | Responsible.
2.tip: Preventive maintenance prevents expensive surprises. Schedule servicing of servers and network equipment on your calendar, just as you would a company car inspection.
2.effort: 8–12 hours | One person | No additional cost

3.description: Monitor third-party maintenance equipment, inspect maintenance devices, ensure safe disposal of assets and verify safety controls after maintenance.
indicators: Monitor the use of third-party maintenance equipment on the company's assets (recording who accessed it, when and to which systems).
Inspect and protect third-party maintenance equipment and storage devices with malicious code detection solutions before connecting them to systems.
Include in the Asset Lifecycle Management Policy measures for the safe disposal of assets (delete data according to the information classification).
After each maintenance, update or repair, check that security controls remain active and functional.
Record the results of checks and take corrective action if necessary.
evidence: Record of use of third-party maintenance equipment (Excel or PDF) with columns: Date | Supplier | Technical | Asset accessed | Equipment used | Inspection carried out (yes/no).
Excerpt from the Asset Lifecycle Management Policy with measures for safe disposal, approved by management.
Post-maintenance security control verification log (Excel or PDF) with: Active | Maintenance carried out | Checks checked | Result | Corrective measures (if applicable).
3.tip: Before throwing away a computer or disk, use a secure erase tool (such as DBAN) to ensure that no data is recoverable. Formatting is not enough.
3.effort: 2–4 hours per maintenance/disposal | An IT person | Cost of tools (most free)

ID.GA-8.title: Data is destroyed in accordance with the defined Data Classification and Management Policy
ID.GA-8.description: The company guarantees that data is destroyed securely when it is no longer necessary, using appropriate methods for classifying information (e.g. secure erasure of files, physical destruction of disks).

2.description: Ensure that critical systems data and sensitive information are destroyed in accordance with the Data Classification and Management Policy.
indicators: Identify critical systems data and sensitive information that require secure destruction.

Include in the Data Classification and Management Policy the rules and destruction methods for each category of data (e.g. secure erasure for digital data, destruction in a shredder for physical documents).
Apply defined destruction methods whenever sensitive data or critical systems are no longer needed.
Record each data destruction action.
evidence: Excerpt from the Data Classification and Management Policy (PDF) with data destruction section: Data category | Destruction method | Responsible.
Data destruction log (Excel or PDF) with columns: Data destroyed | Method used | Date | Responsible, signed by the IT manager.
2.tip: For confidential paper documents, use a cross-cut shredder. For disks and USB sticks, use certified erasure or physical destruction tools.
2.effort: 2–4 hours (definition) + time per action | One person | Cost: shredder (~50-100€) if you don't have one

3.description: Document and test physical and digital sanitization (secure erasure) processes, ensuring the effectiveness of information destruction.
indicators: Document sanitization processes for physical media (e.g. disk destruction, demagnetization) and digital media (e.g. secure erasure with certified software, data overwriting).
Test the sanitization processes to verify that the information was effectively destroyed (e.g. trying to recover data after deletion).
Record the tests carried out and their results.
Update processes if testing reveals failures.
evidence: PDF document with the defined sanitization processes: Type of support | Sanitization method | Tool used | Responsible.
Record of sanitation tests carried out (Excel or PDF) with columns: Test date | Support tested | Method used | Result (unrecoverable data yes/no) | Responsible.
3.tip: Tools like Eraser (free) or DBAN allow you to erase data in a secure and certified way. After deletion, try to recover the data with a tool like Recuva to validate.
3.effort: 4–6 hours | An IT person | No additional cost (free tools)

ID.GA-9.title: The entity implements a change management process
ID.GA-9.description: The company creates formal rules to manage all changes to its systems (updates, new configurations, new software, changes to permissions), ensuring that they are evaluated, approved and tested before being applied.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Establish internal rules for change management, including assessment, pre-approval and testing before implementation in production. Update procedures according to changes made.
indicators: Define the change management process, including: who can request changes, who evaluates them, who approves them and how they are tested before going to production.
Create a change request form or record (with description, reason, expected impact, rollback plan).
Establish that all changes must be tested and validated in a separate environment before being applied to operating systems.
Keep procedures and technical documentation updated after each change.
Record all changes made, including test results.
evidence: PDF document with the change management process: Steps | Responsible | Approval rules | Mandatory testing, approved by management.
Change log (Excel or system) with columns: Request date | Description | Requester | Approver | Test result | Implementation date | Responsible.
Example of a procedure updated after a change, demonstrating that the documentation follows the changes.
3.tip: Even in a small company, keep a simple record: date, what was changed, by whom and why. In case of problems, this record allows you to quickly revert.
3.effort: 6–10 hours (setup) + 15 min per change | An IT person | No additional cost

ID.AR-1.title: Asset vulnerabilities are identified and documented based on the defined methodology
ID.AR-1.description: The company identifies and records the weaknesses (vulnerabilities) of its systems and equipment, to be able to treat them before they are exploited by attackers.

1.description: Identify the vulnerabilities of the entity's assets and document them.
indicators: For each critical asset (servers, applications, network equipment), check whether there are known vulnerabilities (e.g. consult manufacturer alerts, CNCS warnings, public databases such as CVE).
Record each vulnerability found, indicating the affected asset, severity and date of identification.
Define who is responsible for monitoring and addressing each vulnerability.
Update the registry regularly or whenever new vulnerabilities emerge.
evidence: Excel sheet with columns: Active | Vulnerability | Severity (high/medium/low) | Identification date | Responsible | Status (open/under treatment/resolved), signed by the IT manager.
Screen print of alerts consulted (e.g. CNCS website, manufacturer notices) as source of identification.
1.tip: Subscribe to security alerts from CNCS (cert.pt) and the manufacturers of your equipment and software. This way you receive vulnerabilities directly in your email.
1.effort: 2–4 hours per scan | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Approve and implement a Policy for Monitoring, Identification and Documentation of Vulnerabilities of critical systems, with a formal process included in the policy.
