indicators: Write the Vulnerabilities Monitoring, Identification and Documentation Policy, defining: scope (critical systems), sources of information, identification process, documentation rules and frequency.
Define the operational process: how vulnerabilities are collected, who analyzes them, how they are prioritized and how they are treated.
Submit the policy for management approval.
Implement the process, applying it to critical systems.
Record the execution of the process and the results obtained.
evidence: Vulnerability Monitoring, Identification and Documentation Policy in PDF with sections: Scope | Sources | Process | Periodicity | Responsible, approved by management.
Record of process execution (Excel or PDF) showing vulnerabilities identified, analyzed and treated in critical systems.
2.tip: Set a minimum monthly frequency to check critical systems for vulnerabilities. Automate what you can with free vulnerability analysis tools like OpenVAS.
2.effort: 6–10 hours | One person | No additional cost

3.description: Use identification tools and continuous monitoring of vulnerabilities. Perform regular penetration testing.
indicators: Select and implement a tool for identifying and continuously monitoring vulnerabilities (e.g. OpenVAS, Nessus, Qualys).
Configure the tool to regularly analyze all of the company's critical assets.
Analyze the reports generated by the tool and prioritize corrections.
Hire or carry out intrusion tests (pentests) internally at a defined frequency (e.g. annually).
Record penetration test results and implement recommended fixes.
evidence: Screenshot of the vulnerability monitoring tool showing the configuration and results of recent scans.
Last Penetration Test Report (PDF) with: Date | Scope | Vulnerabilities found | Recommendations | Responsible for correction.
3.tip: OpenVAS (Greenbone) is a free and powerful vulnerability analysis tool. For intrusion testing, hire a certified professional; Don't try to do it internally without experience.
3.effort: 8–16 hours (setup) + cost of external pen test | An IT person | Variable cost (pentest: from ~€2,000)

ID.AR-2.title: The entity shares information about cybersecurity threats with interested parties
ID.AR-2.description: The company establishes contact with cybersecurity groups and communities to exchange information about threats, good practices and alerts relevant to its sector of activity.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: Establish contact with interest groups on cybersecurity, exchange experiences on good practices and access relevant information about threats.
indicators: Identify groups, forums or communities for sharing cybersecurity information relevant to the company's sector (e.g. CNCS, sectoral ISACs, user groups, specialized forums).
Subscribe the company or IT manager to these groups or distribution lists.
Define who is responsible for receiving, filtering and sharing relevant threat information internally.
Record the sources used and the most relevant information received.
evidence: List of subscribed cybersecurity information groups/sources (PDF or Excel) with columns: Source | Type (forum/list/community) | Registration date | Internal responsible.
Screenshot of emails or alerts received from threat intelligence sharing sources.
4.tip: Start by subscribing to CNCS alerts (cert.pt) and, if possible, participate in free CNCS webinars on current threats. It's the simplest way to stay informed in Portugal.
4.effort: 1–2 hours (sign-up) + 30 min per week | One person | No additional cost

ID.AR-3.title: Internal and external threats are identified and documented based on the defined methodology
ID.AR-3.description: The company identifies and records threats that may affect its assets, both internal (e.g. human errors, malicious actions by personnel) and external (e.g. computer attacks, natural disasters), following its risk management methodology.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: Identify and document potential internal and external threats that could exploit vulnerabilities in the entity's assets, in accordance with the risk management methodology.
indicators: Define the categories of threats to consider: internal (human errors, sabotage, negligence) and external (computer attacks, viruses, fraud, natural disasters).
For each asset or group of critical assets, identify the most relevant threats.
Record each threat in the risk register, indicating: type (internal/external), origin (natural/human), intentionality (accidental/intentional), affected asset.
Review the list of threats periodically or whenever there are relevant changes in the company's context.
evidence: Excel sheet or risk register section with columns: Threat | Type (internal/external) | Origin (natural/human) | Intentionality | Affected asset(s) | Identification date | Responsible, signed by the IT manager.
Record of the last revision date of the threat list.
4.tip: Insider threats are often underestimated. Don't forget to include scenarios such as: employee who sends data in error, lost USB stick, or former employee with still active access.
4.effort: 3–6 hours | One person | No additional cost

ID.AR-4.title: Potential impacts and respective threat probabilities are identified and recorded
ID.AR-4.description: The company defines criteria to assess the impact and probability of each threat materializing, in order to calculate the level of risk associated with each one.

1.description: Determine and record the probability of threats materializing and assess their potential impacts, within the risk management process.
indicators: Define a probability scale (e.g. 1=Very unlikely, 2=Unlikely, 3=Possible, 4=Likely, 5=Very likely).
Define an impact scale (e.g. 1=Negligible, 2=Minor, 3=Moderate, 4=Serious, 5=Very serious).
For each identified threat, assign probability and impact values, based on company knowledge and the sector context.
Record these values ​​in the risk register, together with the justification for the assigned classification.
evidence: PDF document with the criteria for determining probability and impact: Probability scale | Description of each level | Scale of impact | Description of each level.
Risk register (Excel) with columns: Threat | Probability | Impact | Justification | Resulting risk level | Date | Responsible, signed by the IT manager.
1.tip: Use scales of 1 to 5 for probability and impact. The risk level is simply probability times impact. This way you can easily prioritize: the risks with the highest score are dealt with first.
1.effort: 3–5 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Substantial

ID.AR-5.title: The entity assesses the identified risks
ID.AR-5.description: The company formally assesses cybersecurity risks, combining information about threats, vulnerabilities, probabilities and impacts to determine the level of risk and decide how to address them.

1.description: Carry out risk assessments based on the analysis of threats, vulnerabilities, probabilities and impacts on all assets that guarantee the continued functioning of the systems.
indicators: Gather the information already collected: list of assets, identified vulnerabilities, threats, probabilities and impacts.
For each critical asset, cross-reference threats with vulnerabilities and calculate the risk level (probability x impact).
List all risks found, ordered by severity level.
Record the risk assessment in a formal document.
Share results with management to inform decision making.
evidence: Risk assessment record (Excel or PDF) with columns: Active | Threat | Vulnerability | Probability | Impact | Risk level | Date | Responsible, signed by the IT manager.
Record of communication of results to management (email or meeting minutes).
1.tip: Risk assessment does not need to be an academic exercise. Use a simple Excel sheet with the indicated columns and fill it out with common sense. The most important thing is to do, not to be perfect.
1.effort: 4–8 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Risk assessments must be conducted, documented and updated continuously, based on the criteria defined in the risk management methodology.
indicators: Apply the risk management methodology defined in the policy to conduct risk assessment in a structured and repeatable manner.
Formally document each risk assessment carried out, including the criteria used (according to the methodology).
Define a periodicity for updating the risk assessment (e.g. biannual or annual).
Update the assessment whenever significant changes occur (new asset, new service, incident, new threat).
Maintain a history of assessments to monitor the evolution of risk over time.
evidence: Documented risk assessment (PDF or Excel) with reference to the methodology used, criteria applied and date of completion, signed by the person responsible.
Risk assessment history (version log or tab with dates) showing continuous updates.
2.tip: Mark your calendar with a fixed date to review the risk assessment (e.g. January and July). This ensures that it is done regularly and does not get forgotten.
2.effort: 4–6 hours per review | One person | No additional cost

ID.AR-6.title: The entity ensures that responses to risks are identified and prioritized
ID.AR-6.description: For each risk assessed, the company decides the appropriate response, defines priorities, plans implementation and monitors progress.

1.description: Implement security controls for identified risks.
indicators: For each risk identified in the risk assessment, define at least one appropriate security control (e.g. firewall, antivirus, backup, access restriction).
Implement defined controls, starting with the highest level risks.
Record the controls implemented and associate them with the corresponding risks.
Verify that controls are working correctly after implementation.
evidence: Risk register (Excel) with additional columns: Control implemented | Implementation date | Status | Responsible, signed by the IT manager.
Technical evidence of implemented controls (e.g. active firewall screenshot, antivirus report, backup configuration).
1.tip: Start with the simplest controls that cover more risks at the same time: updated antivirus, active firewall, automatic backups and up-to-date security updates.
1.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | Variable cost (according to controls)

2.description: Identify, prioritize, plan, monitor and communicate responses to risks.
indicators: For each risk in the record, identify the appropriate response (accept, reduce, transfer, eliminate) and prioritize based on the risk level.
Develop a risk treatment plan with deadlines and people responsible for each response.
Monitor the execution of the plan periodically, recording progress.
Communicate the status of risk treatment to management and relevant stakeholders.
evidence: Risk treatment plan (Excel or PDF) with columns: Risk | Answer | Priority | Deadline | Responsible | Status | Date of last update, signed by the person responsible.
Record of communication to management (email or minutes) about the status of risk treatment.
2.tip: Don't try to treat all risks at the same time. Focus on the 3-5 most critical risks first. The rest remain in the plan, scheduled for later treatment.
2.effort: 4–6 hours | One person | No additional cost

3.description: Include in the risk treatment strategy a plan for monitoring, reviewing and continuously improving responses to risks.
indicators: Define indicators to monitor the effectiveness of risk responses (e.g. number of risks resolved, average resolution time, residual risk).
Include in the risk treatment strategy a plan for periodic monitoring of implemented responses.
Periodically review whether the responses remain adequate or whether they need to be adjusted.
Record the revisions and improvements implemented.
Report monitoring results to management.
evidence: Plan for monitoring and continuous improvement of risk responses (PDF) with sections: Indicators | Monitoring frequency | Responsible | Review criteria, integrated into the treatment strategy.
Monitoring record (Excel or PDF) with indicator results and improvement actions implemented.
3.tip: Review risk responses every 6 months. Control that was adequate a year ago may no longer be sufficient if the context has changed (new attacks, new systems, more people).
3.effort: 3–5 hours per review | One person | No additional cost

ID.AR-7.title: The entity defines processes to receive information, analyze and respond to vulnerabilities originating from internal and external sources
ID.AR-7.description: The company has a formal process for receiving information about vulnerabilities (from internal sources such as testing, or external sources such as security researchers), analyzing it, and responding appropriately.

1.description: Treat vulnerabilities of which the entity is aware, recording their identification and treatment.
indicators: Ensure that there is a way to receive information about vulnerabilities (e.g. email from the IT manager, contact on the company website, internal reporting).
When a vulnerability is reported, analyze whether it is valid and whether it affects the company's systems.
If confirmed, register the vulnerability and define the treatment action (correct, mitigate or accept).
Record the treatment applied and the resolution date.
evidence: Record of treated vulnerabilities (Excel) with columns: Vulnerability | Source (internal/external) | Communication date | Valid (yes/no) | Treatment action | Resolution date | Responsible, signed by the IT manager.
Technical evidence of the treatment applied (e.g. print of the applied patch, configuration change).
1.tip: Create a dedicated email address (e.g. security@company.com) to receive vulnerability reports. It's simple and shows that the company takes security seriously.
1.effort: 1–2 hours per vulnerability | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Implement vulnerability management processes that include processing, analysis and correction of internal and external sources, aligned with the National Policy for Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure.
indicators: Define a formal vulnerability management process with the steps: reception, screening, analysis, prioritization, correction and verification.
Identify and document internal and external sources of vulnerability information.
Align the process with the National Policy for Coordinated Disclosure of Vulnerabilities (published by the CNCS).
Implement the process and record all vulnerabilities treated.
Review the process periodically to ensure its effectiveness.
evidence: PDF document with the vulnerability management process: Steps | Sources | Prioritization criteria | Responsible | Alignment with National Policy, approved by management.
Record of processed vulnerabilities (Excel or PDF) with columns: Vulnerability | Source | Current stage | Priority | Correction action | Date | Responsible.
2.tip: Consult the National Policy for Coordinated Disclosure of Vulnerabilities on the CNCS website to understand national obligations and good practices in this matter.
2.effort: 6–10 hours (process definition) | One person | No additional cost

3.description: Systematically analyze and assess vulnerabilities. Implement automated mechanisms to disseminate and track remediation efforts to stakeholders.
indicators: Implement a systematic analysis and assessment of all registered vulnerabilities (e.g. classification by severity using CVSS or equivalent scale).
Select and implement automated tools to track the vulnerability lifecycle (registration, prioritization, assignment, monitoring, closure).
Configure automatic notifications to stakeholders about patch status.
Record the results of the systematic analysis and the corrections made.
Produce periodic reports on the status of vulnerabilities.
evidence: Screenshot of the automated vulnerability management tool showing the lifecycle of at least one vulnerability (from registration to closure).
Record of the systematic analysis and assessment of vulnerabilities (PDF or Excel) with severity classification and prioritization.
Example of automatic notification sent to stakeholders about remediation status.
3.tip: Tools like DefectDojo (free and open-source) allow you to manage the entire life cycle of vulnerabilities, from identification to correction, with automatic notifications.
3.effort: 8–16 hours (implementation) | An IT person | Cost: free (open-source tools) or variable

ID.MC-1.title: Improvements are identified through assessments and information is shared with interested parties
ID.MC-1.description: The company identifies opportunities to improve its cybersecurity through assessments, lessons learned from past incidents, and shares this information with relevant parties to reduce the risk of future incidents.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: Identify improvements through assessments and lessons learned sessions from incidents that have occurred, and share this information with relevant parties.
indicators: Define that, after each security assessment or incident, a lessons learned session is held.
In each session, identify what worked well, what failed and what can be improved.
Record the improvements identified and assign those responsible for their implementation.
Share lessons learned with relevant internal stakeholders.
Monitor the implementation of identified improvements.
evidence: Lessons learned session log (PDF or Excel) with columns: Date | Event (incident/evaluation) | What worked | What Failed | Improvement identified | Responsible | Deadline, signed by the person responsible for cybersecurity.
Email or minutes sharing lessons learned with interested parties.
4.tip: Conduct the lessons learned session within 5 days of the incident or assessment, while the information is still fresh in the minds of everyone involved.
4.effort: 2–3 hours per session | One person | No additional cost

ID.MC-2.title: Improvements are identified from cybersecurity tests and exercises
ID.MC-2.description: The company conducts cybersecurity tests and exercises (including with external stakeholders) to identify weaknesses and opportunities for improvement in its plans and processes.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Use cybersecurity tests and exercises, including with interested parties, to identify points for improvement in plans and processes.
indicators: Plan and schedule periodic cybersecurity tests and exercises (e.g. incident simulation, disaster recovery exercise, continuity plan test).
When applicable, involve external stakeholders (key suppliers, partners) in the exercises.
Carry out tests and exercises, recording the results and difficulties encountered.
Analyze the results and identify areas for improvement.
Update plans and processes with identified improvements.
evidence: Plan and record of tests/exercises performed (PDF) with sections: Date | Scenario | Participants (internal and external) | Results | Points for improvement identified | Improvement actions, signed by the person responsible for cybersecurity.
Updated version of plans or processes with improvements incorporated (demonstrating the before/after).
3.tip: Start with tabletop exercises (a meeting where a scenario is simulated and the answers are discussed, without touching the systems). They are cheap, quick and extremely revealing.
3.effort: 4–8 hours per exercise | One to two people | No additional cost

ID.MC-3.title: Improvements are identified through the execution of processes, procedures and operational activities
ID.MC-3.description: The company regularly evaluates and updates its cybersecurity processes, extracts lessons from incidents that occur and identifies operational improvements to strengthen its protection and detection capacity.

1.description: Conduct post-incident assessments to identify lessons learned and improve processes, procedures and technologies to strengthen cyber resilience.
indicators: After each cybersecurity incident, conduct a post-incident assessment (meeting with everyone involved).
Identify the causes of the incident, what worked in the response and what can be improved.
Define concrete improvement actions (e.g. update a procedure, install a tool, reinforce a configuration).
Implement improvement actions and record their execution.
Check, in the next incident, whether the improvements implemented were effective.
evidence: Post-incident assessment report (PDF) with sections: Incident date | Description | Causes | What worked | What Failed | Defined improvements | Responsible | Deadline | Implementation status, signed by the IT manager.
Record of implementation of defined improvements (Excel or PDF) proving that the actions were carried out.
1.tip: Do not skip post-incident assessments even for minor incidents. Often, small incidents reveal the same failures that later cause big problems.
1.effort: 2–4 hours per assessment | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Include in cybersecurity management plans and processes improvements derived from periodic monitoring and evaluation of controls and processes.
indicators: Define a periodicity to evaluate the effectiveness of cybersecurity controls and processes in place (e.g. every six months).
Carry out the assessment, identifying processes that need improvement and controls that are not working as expected.
Record identified improvements and incorporate them into cybersecurity management plans and processes.
Monitor the implementation of improvements and record progress.
evidence: Periodic evaluation record (Excel or PDF) with columns: Process/Control evaluated | Effectiveness (adequate/insufficient) | Improvement identified | Date | Responsible.
Updated version of cybersecurity plans or processes, incorporating identified improvements (demonstrate the changes made).
2.tip: Schedule a cybersecurity 'health check' every 6 months. Go through the controls in place and ask: are they still working? Are they still relevant? Is there something new we should be doing?
2.effort: 4–6 hours per assessment | One person | No additional cost

PR.GA-1.title: Identity management lifecycles are defined
PR.GA-1.description: The entity ensures that access accounts to its systems (who enters, with what credentials and when) are created, maintained, reviewed and deactivated in an organized manner.

1.description: The identities and credentials of devices, services and users must be managed. Implement a Password Policy and an Identity Management, Authentication and Access Control Policy.
indicators: Create a centralized list of all user accounts, service accounts, and devices that access the entity's systems.
Define and approve a Password Policy (minimum size, complexity, validity period, prohibition of reuse).
Define and approve an Identity Management, Authentication and Access Control Policy with rules for creating, changing and deactivating accounts.
Apply policy rules to systems (Active Directory, Microsoft 365, internal applications).
Review the list of active accounts quarterly and deactivate those that are no longer necessary.
evidence: Password Policy in PDF, with fields: minimum size, complexity, expiration, reuse, approved and signed by management.
Identity Management, Authentication and Access Control Policy in PDF, with sections: creating accounts, changing permissions, deactivating accounts, responsible for approval. Signed by management.
Screenshot from Active Directory or equivalent system showing the applied password policies.
Folha Excel ou print do sistema com lista de contas ativas, com colunas: Nome | Type (user/service/device) | Creation date | Status | Last revision.
1.tip: In Active Directory or Microsoft 365 Admin Center, you can configure password rules on a single page. Start there before creating any documents.
1.effort: 4–8 hours | One to two people (IT + management) | No additional cost

2.description: Credentials should be automatically deactivated after a period of inactivity defined in the policy. Critical systems must be monitored to detect atypical uses of credentials.
indicators: Configure the systems to automatically deactivate accounts after the period of inactivity defined in the Identity Management Policy (e.g. 90 days without login).
Identify systems classified as critical and activate alerts for atypical behavior (out-of-hours logins, access from unexpected locations, repeated failed attempts).
Define who receives and analyzes atypical usage alerts.
Register and track alerts in a sheet or ticket system.
evidence: Screenshot of the automatic account deactivation configuration due to inactivity in Active Directory or equivalent system, showing the defined period.
Screenshot of alerts configured for atypical use of credentials in critical systems (e.g. Microsoft Sentinel, Windows audit rules).
Excel sheet or ticket system entry with columns: Alert date | System | Type of anomaly | Responsible for analysis | Action taken.
2.tip: In Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD) there is the 'Sign-in risk policy' option that automatically detects suspicious logins, even in the free version with limitations.
2.effort: 4–6 hours | An IT person | No additional cost (Microsoft tools included)

3.description: Identities and credentials must be managed by automated mechanisms. Secure authentication (MFA, certificates, tokens) must be applied to communications between critical systems.
indicators: Implement a centralized identity management tool that automates the creation, change and removal of accounts (e.g. Microsoft Entra ID, JumpCloud).
Configure automatic flows: when an employee leaves, the account is automatically deactivated.
Apply multi-factor authentication (MFA) in communications between critical systems (e.g. connections between the ERP and the database, between servers).
Implement digital certificates or tokens for authentication between systems, when applicable.
Periodically review automated mechanisms to confirm that they function correctly.
evidence: Screenshot of the automated identity management tool, showing the automatic creation and deactivation flows.
Screenshot of the MFA configuration applied to communications between critical systems.
Screenshot or report showing digital certificates or tokens in use for cross-system authentication.
PDF record of the last revision of automated mechanisms, with date and person responsible.
3.tip: If you already use Microsoft 365, Entra ID allows you to automate a large part of identity management at no additional cost, including integration with the employee lifecycle.
3.effort: 8–16 hours | One to two IT people | Possible additional licensing cost

PR.GA-2.title: The entity verifies users' identity and links it to their credentials
PR.GA-2.description: Before giving access to someone, the entity confirms that that person is really who they say they are and only then gives them the access credentials.

2.description: Implement procedures, in accordance with the Identity Management Policy, to verify the identity of users before issuing access credentials.
indicators: Define the identity verification procedure to be applied before handing over credentials (e.g. in-person validation, sending to personal email confirmed by HR).
Document the procedure as part of the Identity Management, Authentication and Access Control Policy.
Apply the procedure whenever new accounts are created or credentials are reset.
Record each check carried out (who checked, when and what method they used).
evidence: Section of the Identity Management, Authentication and Access Control Policy (PDF) with the identity verification procedure before issuing credentials.
Excel sheet or system record with columns: User name | Order date | Verification method used | Who checked | Date of issuance of credentials.
2.tip: The simplest method is: HR confirms via internal email that employee X has joined the service, and only then does IT create the account. Never create accounts based on a phone call alone.
2.effort: 2–4 hours | Two people (HR + IT) | No additional cost

PR.GA-3.title: Authentication mechanisms for users, devices and other information system assets are defined
PR.GA-3.description: The entity defines how users and devices prove their identity when accessing systems (e.g. password + code on cell phone).

1.description: Implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) on the entity's devices and information systems.
indicators: Identify all systems and devices that support multi-factor authentication (MFA), that is, the use of two or more methods to confirm identity (e.g. password + code on the cell phone).
Activate MFA for all users, starting with the most critical access (email, VPN, system administration).
Communicate to employees what MFA is and how they should configure it on their devices.
Confirm that MFA is active for all users and record the status.
evidence: Screenshot of the administration panel (e.g. Microsoft 365 Admin, Google Workspace) showing that MFA is active for all users.
Communication sent to employees (PDF email) with instructions for configuring MFA.
1.tip: In Microsoft 365, you can enable MFA for your entire organization in less than 10 minutes via the Security > MFA dashboard. Do not delay this measure: it is the most effective protection against credential theft.
1.effort: 2–4 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

2.description: Carry out a risk assessment of communications between critical systems and apply authentication mechanisms proportionate to the identified risk.
indicators: List all communications between critical systems (e.g. ERP with database, file server with backup system).
Assess the risk of each connection, considering the sensitivity of the data and the exposure of the communication.
Apply authentication mechanisms appropriate to the risk (e.g. digital certificates for high-risk connections, MFA for administrative access).
Document the decisions made for each communication.
evidence: Excel sheet with columns: System A | System B | Data type | Risk level | Authentication mechanism applied | Responsible for the decision.
Screenshot of authentication mechanisms configured between critical systems (e.g. TLS certificates, API keys).
2.tip: Start with connections that involve personal or financial data. If two systems exchange sensitive information without any authentication between them, this is the most urgent risk to deal with.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | One to two IT people | No additional cost

3.description: Communications between the entity's systems and external systems must be framed in a formal agreement between the parties.
indicators: Identify all communications between internal systems and external systems (suppliers, partners, cloud services).
For each connection, check whether there is a written agreement regulating communication security requirements.
Where there is no agreement, develop a model that includes the authentication, encryption requirements and responsibilities of each party.
Formalize agreements with signatures from both parties.
evidence: Agreement template in PDF with sections: parties involved, systems covered, required authentication mechanisms, responsibilities, date and signatures.
Excel sheet with columns: External entity | External system | Internal system | Existing agreement (Yes/No) | Agreement date | Next review.
3.tip: This agreement may be a technical annex to the main contract with the supplier. It doesn't need to be an extensive document, it just needs to clearly define how and who authenticates.
3.effort: 6–10 hours | An IT + legal/management person | No additional cost

PR.GA-4.title: Identity claims are protected, transmitted and verified
PR.GA-4.description: When a system relies on authentication from another system (e.g. single sign-on/SSO), these digital 'proofs of identity' must be protected from being tampered with or intercepted.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Digital proofs of identity (tokens, assertions) exchanged between systems must be protected, transmitted securely and verified by the receiving system.
indicators: Identify systems that use single sign-on (SSO) mechanisms or that receive authentication information from other systems (SAML, OAuth, OpenID Connect tokens).
Check that identity proofs are transmitted only via encrypted channels (HTTPS/TLS).
Configure receiving systems to validate the digital signature and the temporal validity of the identity proofs received.
Define a maximum validity time for each proof of identity (e.g. token valid for 1 hour).
Register and periodically review the configuration of SSO mechanisms.
evidence: Screenshot of the SSO system configuration showing encrypted channels (HTTPS) and validation of digital signatures.
Screenshot of the tokens' temporal validity parameters (e.g. Azure AD configuration or other identity provider).
PDF record of the last revision of the SSO configuration, with date and person responsible.
3.tip: If you use Microsoft Entra ID or Google Workspace as your identity provider, identity proof protection is already configured by default. Just check that you have not changed the default values ​​to less safe values.
3.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

PR.GA-5.title: Access permissions, rights and authorizations are defined in a policy, managed, applied and updated
PR.GA-5.description: The entity defines who can access what, ensures that each person has only the permissions necessary for their work (least privilege) and that no single person controls a critical process from end to end (separation of duties).

1.description: Access permissions must be defined and managed according to the Identity Management Policy. Apply the principle of least privilege and do not assign administration privileges by default.
indicators: Create an access matrix that associates each company function (position) with the systems and data it needs to access.
Review all existing accounts and remove permissions that exceed what is necessary for each role.
Ensure that no regular user accounts have administration privileges.
Document and approve all accounts with administration privileges, justifying the need.
Schedule a periodic review (e.g. quarterly) of assigned permissions.
evidence: Excel sheet with the access matrix, with columns: Function/Position | System | Access level | Justification. Validated by the IT manager.
Excel sheet with the list of users with administration privileges, with columns: Name | System | Justification | Approved by | Approval date.
Section of the Identity Management Policy (PDF) that describes the principle of least privilege and the prohibition of default administration privileges.
Record of the last periodic review of permissions (date, who did it, changes made).
1.tip: Start with the most dangerous scenario: someone who has left the company and still has active access. Then, check if any common user has unnecessary administrator access.
1.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

2.description: Apply automated mechanisms to manage access to critical systems, include segregation of duties and regularly manage and review elevated privileges.
indicators: Implement automation for managing the life cycle of accounts in critical systems (automatic creation, deactivation and deletion when there are changes in the staffing table).
Apply separation of functions in critical processes (e.g. those who request access are not those who approve it; those who make a transfer are not those who authorize it).
Document the separation of functions in a table that associates each critical process with the different people involved.
Create a registry of all users with elevated privileges (system administrators, databases, network) and review this list monthly.
Monitor the assignment of new elevated privileges and require formal approval on a case-by-case basis.
evidence: Screen print of automations configured for account management in critical systems (e.g. flows in Microsoft Entra ID, automatic deactivation scripts).
Excel sheet with the segregation of duties table: Process | Step 1 (who) | Step 2 (who) | Step 3 (who).
Excel sheet with list of users with elevated privileges, with columns: Name | System | Privilege | Award date | Approval (by whom and when) | Last revision.
Record of the monthly review of elevated privileges, with date, changes and person responsible.
2.tip: Segregation of duties does not require a large team. In an SME of 10 people, it is enough to ensure that the person requesting access is not the same person who assigns it in the system.
2.effort: 6–10 hours | One to two IT people | No additional cost

3.description: Monitor the context in which authentication occurs (device, location, time) and monitor the activity of users with elevated privileges.
indicators: Configure rules that detect authentications from unusual devices, locations or times (e.g. login at 3am, login from a different country).
Define automatic actions for these situations (e.g. block access, require additional MFA, send alert).
Enable detailed monitoring of actions performed by users with elevated privileges (who did what and when on critical systems).
Periodically review administrator activity logs to detect anomalous behavior.
evidence: Screenshot of configured conditional access rules (e.g. Microsoft Entra Conditional Access) showing restrictions by device, location and time.
Screen print of alerts configured for access outside the usual pattern.
Screen print or report monitoring activities of users with elevated privileges (e.g. Azure AD or SIEM logs).
PDF record of the periodic review of administrator logs, with date, findings and person responsible.
3.tip: Microsoft Entra ID Conditional Access allows you to block logins from countries where the company does not operate, simply and at no additional cost on most M365 Business Premium licenses.
3.effort: 6–10 hours | An IT person | Possible licensing cost for advanced features

PR.GA-6.title: Physical access controls to the entity's assets are managed, monitored and applied according to risk
PR.GA-6.description: The entity controls who physically enters the areas where servers, network equipment and confidential information are located.

1.description: Define which are the restricted access areas (where servers, network racks, confidential information are located) and protect them.
indicators: Identify all areas that contain servers, network equipment, racks, or confidential documentation.
Classify these areas as restricted access zones and sign with visible identification.
Apply physical protection measures (e.g. door with lock or card, controlled key).
Define who is authorized to access each zone and register that list.
evidence: Installation plan or diagram (PDF) with restricted access areas marked.
Excel sheet with the list of restricted access zones and, for each one: Zone | Location | Type of protection | Authorized persons.
Photograph or screenshot of signage and access mechanisms (lock, card, code).
1.tip: If the server is under a desk in an open room, consider moving it to a room with a lockable door. It is the simplest and most effective measure to start with.
1.effort: 2–4 hours | An IT + management person | Variable cost (lock, if none exists)

2.description: Physical access to restricted areas must be monitored (entry/exit registration) and measures must be in place for emergency situations.
indicators: Implement entry and exit registration in restricted access areas (manual log book, card or code system).
Define access measures for emergency situations (e.g. sealed emergency key, emergency access procedure, alternative contact person).
Document emergency measures and communicate them to relevant people.
Periodically review access logs to detect unauthorized or out-of-hours access.
evidence: Access control system log or export book with columns: Date | Check-in time | Departure time | Person | Zone | Reason.
PDF document with access measures in emergency situations, validated by the IT manager.
Record of the last revision of access logs, with date, findings and person responsible.
2.tip: If you don't have an electronic system, a simple log book outside the server room will do the trick. The important thing is that there is a consultable register.
2.effort: 2–4 hours | One person | No additional cost (or registration book cost)

3.description: Assets within restricted access zones must be physically protected with additional mechanisms.
indicators: Install additional physical protections for assets within restricted areas (e.g. lockable racks/racks, security cables for portable equipment).
Check that network racks and servers are locked and only accessible to authorized personnel.
Consider additional protections such as surveillance cameras inside critical rooms.
Document the protections in place and periodically review their status.
evidence: Photograph or screenshot of locked racks and other physical protections installed.
Excel sheet with columns: Active | Zone | Applied physical protection | Verification date | State.
Screen print or video surveillance system record (if applicable), showing coverage of critical areas.
3.tip: A rack with a lockable door costs less than €200 and protects servers from unauthorized physical access, accidental shutdowns and manipulation.
3.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | Variable cost (backstage, cameras)

PR.GA-7.title: The entity manages its remote accesses
PR.GA-7.description: The entity controls and protects access to its systems when this is done remotely (e.g. working from home, access from outside the office).

1.description: Define and document the list of users and devices allowed remote access and implement appropriate security controls.
indicators: List all users and devices that remotely access the entity's systems.
Formally approve this list (who can access remotely and which systems).
Implement a VPN connection (virtual private network, which creates a secure 'tunnel' between the remote computer and the company network) or other secure remote access mechanism.
Ensure MFA is active for all remote access.
Disable remote access that is no longer necessary.
evidence: Excel sheet with columns: User name | Device | Accessible system(s) | Approval date | Approved by.
Screenshot of the VPN configuration or other secure remote access mechanism.
Screenshot showing that MFA is active for remote access.
1.tip: If the company uses Microsoft 365, remote access to email and files is already protected by default. The focus should be on securing remote access to the internal server or local network.
1.effort: 4–6 hours | An IT person | No additional cost (VPN included with many corporate routers)

2.description: Set remote access restrictions to critical systems, monitor remote maintenance and revoke access when it is no longer necessary.
indicators: Define specific restrictions for remote access to the critical systems environment (allowed times, authorized devices, connection requirements).
Include these restrictions in the Identity Management, Authentication and Access Control Policy.
Monitor all actions carried out by third parties during remote maintenance (record who accessed it, when and what they did).
Immediately revoke access created for remote maintenance as soon as it ends.
Review active remote accesses monthly and remove those that are no longer necessary.
evidence: Section of the Identity Management Policy (PDF) with restrictions on remote access to critical systems.
Screen print of the remote session monitoring system (e.g. VPN logs, remote access software logs).
Excel sheet with columns: Supplier/Technical | Access date | System accessed | Actions taken | Access revoked on (date).
Record of the monthly review of active remote accesses.
2.tip: If you use TeamViewer, AnyDesk or similar for external technicians to carry out maintenance, never leave permanent access active. Create temporary accesses and revoke them when maintenance is complete.
2.effort: 4–6 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

3.description: Remote access to critical systems must be continuously monitored and carried out exclusively through secure channels with strong encryption.
indicators: Ensure that all remote access to critical systems uses encrypted channels (VPN with strong encryption, TLS 1.2 or higher).
Define and internally approve the list of secure protocols allowed for remote access.
Collect and analyze activity logs from remote users when accessing critical systems.
Configure alerts for suspicious activities in remote sessions (transfers of large volumes of data, after-hours access).
Apply the same secure channel rules to remote maintenance actions.
evidence: Screenshot of the VPN configuration showing the type of encryption used (e.g. AES-256, IKEv2).
PDF document with the list of approved protocols, signed by the IT manager.
Screen print or report of activity logs from remote sessions to critical systems.
Screen print of configured alerts for suspicious activities in remote access.
3.tip: Avoid old remote access protocols like RDP exposed directly to the internet. Always use a VPN as an additional layer before any remote access to servers.
3.effort: 6–10 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

PR.FC-1.title: All staff are sensitized and trained in cybersecurity matters
PR.FC-1.description: The entity ensures that all employees participate in awareness raising activities (for example, what phishing is) and training on cybersecurity.

1.description: Ensure that all staff periodically participate in cybersecurity awareness actions.
indicators: Choose or create awareness content adapted to the company's reality (e.g. how to recognize phishing emails, good password practices, care with public Wi-Fi).
Hold at least one awareness session per year for all employees.
Record the presence (or completion, if online) of all participants.
Schedule the following sessions on the company calendar.
evidence: Attendance sheet (PDF or Excel) with columns: Participant name | Session date | Session theme | Signature or confirmation of presence.
Material used in the session (PDF presentation or link to the online course).
Calendar or annual planning of awareness raising actions.
1.tip: There are free cybersecurity awareness platforms (such as Google's 'CyberAware' or CNCS courses). A 30-minute session per quarter, with real examples of attacks, makes a difference.
1.effort: 2–4 hours per session | One person to organize | No additional cost

2.description: Conduct periodic training so that staff understand their roles during an incident and know how to recognize internal threats.
indicators: Include in the training a module on what each person should do in the event of a cybersecurity incident (who to report it to, what steps to take, what not to do).
Include a module on insider threats: what they are, practical examples and how to report suspicious behavior.
Carry out training periodically (e.g. every six months).
Record participation and topics covered in each session.
evidence: Training material (PDF) with modules on incident response and internal threats.
Attendance sheet (PDF or Excel) with columns: Name | Date | Modules completed | Signature.
Calendar of periodic training approved by management.
2.tip: Use real examples of incidents (public news of attacks on Portuguese companies) to make the training more concrete. People retain stories better than lists of rules.
2.effort: 3–6 hours per session | One to two people | No additional cost

3.description: Define indicators to measure the effectiveness of awareness and training actions, and use the results to continually improve.
indicators: Define indicators to evaluate effectiveness (e.g. percentage of employees who clicked on phishing test emails, participation rate, results of post-training questionnaires).
Collect the results of the indicators after each training action.
Analyze the results and identify areas that need strengthening.
Adjust the content and frequency of training based on the results.
Document the improvements applied and the evolution of indicators over time.
evidence: PDF document with the definition of effectiveness indicators (what they measure, how they are collected, expected target).
Excel sheet or report with the results collected, with columns: Indicator | Measurement date | Result | Goal | Detour.
PDF record of improvements applied to training based on results, signed by the person responsible.
3.tip: A phishing simulation (sending a controlled fake email and counting how many people clicked) is the most effective and easiest indicator to implement. There are free tools like GoPhish.
3.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost (with open source tools)

PR.FC-2.title: Personnel with specialized cybersecurity functions receive appropriate training to carry out their duties
PR.FC-2.description: People who have cybersecurity responsibilities (including managers and administrators) receive specific and updated training to perform their role well.

1.description: Members of management, management and administration bodies periodically complete cybersecurity training.
indicators: Identify the members of management, management and administration bodies who must participate in cybersecurity training.
Enroll these members in periodic training appropriate to their level (focus on business risks, legal responsibilities, decision making).
Ensure that each member completes at least one training per year.
Record the participation of each member.
evidence: Excel sheet or record with columns: Name | Position | Training carried out | Date | Training entity | Certificate (Yes/No).
Training certificates or declarations of participation in PDF.
1.tip: CNCS offers free online cybersecurity courses for managers. Training of 2 to 4 hours per year is sufficient for basic level.
1.effort: 2–4 hours per person | No additional cost (free courses available)

2.description: Define, implement and update a structured cybersecurity training plan for staff with specialized functions.
indicators: Identify all personnel with specialized cybersecurity roles (system administrators, IT manager, security manager).
Create an annual training plan specific to these functions, with themes, dates and suppliers.
Execute the training plan and record all actions carried out.
Review and update the plan annually based on new threats and needs identified.
evidence: Training plan in PDF with sections: personnel covered, topics, calendar, suppliers, budget. Approved by management.
Excel sheet with columns: Name | Function | Training | Completion date | Certificate | Next formation.
Record of annual review of the plan, with date and changes made.
2.tip: For technical staff, platforms such as Coursera, Udemy or SANS offer specialized training. Include at least one new topic per year (e.g. cloud security, incident response).
2.effort: 4–8 hours of planning | One person | Variable cost (external training)

PR.SD-1.title: The entity protects the confidentiality, integrity and availability of data at rest
PR.SD-1.description: Data stored in the entity's systems (files, databases, backup copies) are protected against unauthorized access, unauthorized changes and loss.

2.description: Implement integrity checks of information, software and firmware to detect unauthorized changes. Restrict the use of removable storage devices in accordance with the Acceptable Use Policy.
indicators: Activate integrity checking mechanisms in systems (e.g. hash check of critical files, operating system integrity monitoring with tools such as OSSEC or Windows File Integrity Monitoring).
Configure alerts when unauthorized changes to files, software or firmware are detected.
Restrict the use of USB flash drives and other removable storage devices at workstations, as defined in the Acceptable Use Policy.
Document authorized exceptions for use of removable devices.
evidence: Screenshot of the integrity check tool configuration (e.g. OSSEC, Wazuh or Windows Group Policy).
Screen print of the USB device restriction configuration on workstations (e.g. GPO in Active Directory).
Excel sheet of authorized exceptions with columns: User | Device | Justification | Approved by | Date.
2.tip: On Windows, you can lock USB flash drives using a group policy (GPO) in less than 15 minutes. It is a simple measure that greatly reduces the risk of data leakage or virus entry.
2.effort: 4–6 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

3.description: Protect critical/sensitive information stored in critical systems with additional controls (e.g. encryption, granular access control).
indicators: Identify critical and sensitive data stored in critical systems (personal, financial data, intellectual property).
Apply encryption to the disks or folders that contain this data (e.g. BitLocker on Windows, database encryption).
Implement granular access controls (only those strictly necessary can access that data).
Periodically review that controls remain active and effective.
evidence: Screenshot of BitLocker or equivalent tool active on systems that store critical data.
Screenshot of granular access permissions configured for folders or databases with sensitive information.
PDF record of the periodic review of controls, with date, status and person responsible.
3.tip: BitLocker is included with Pro and Enterprise versions of Windows and can be activated without installing anything. Remember to store the recovery key in a separate, safe location.
3.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

PR.SD-2.title: The entity protects the confidentiality, integrity and availability of data in transit
PR.SD-2.description: Data that circulates between systems (internal or external) is protected so that no one can read or change it during transmission.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Adopt security controls to protect data in transit in accordance with its classification and the Data Classification and Management Policy, including data on removable media.
indicators: Ensure that all communications between systems use encryption (HTTPS, TLS 1.2 or higher, VPN for connections between headquarters).
Check servers' digital certificates and renew them before they expire.
When sensitive data is transferred on removable media (USB sticks, external disks), ensure that these media are encrypted.
Apply controls proportionate to data classification (more sensitive data requires stronger encryption).
Document the controls applied and exceptions accepted.
evidence: Screenshot of TLS certificates installed on web servers and applications, showing the protocol version and validity.
Screenshot of encryption configuration for VPN connections between locations.
Excel sheet with columns: Type of communication | Data transmitted | Classification | Encryption control applied | Exception (if applicable).
Screenshot of the removable media encryption tool (e.g. BitLocker To Go).
3.tip: Check with your browser whether the company's internal websites use 'https://' (with padlock). If anyone still uses 'http://', ​​they are transmitting data in a readable form to anyone on the network.
3.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

PR.SD-3.title: The entity implements protections that prevent data exfiltration
PR.SD-3.description: The entity applies controls to prevent data from leaving the organization in an unauthorized way (for example, sending confidential files abroad).

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Adopt security controls to monitor critical systems at external borders and internal critical points, to prevent unauthorized access and data exit.
indicators: Identify the network's external borders (internet connections, connections with partners) and internal critical points (borders between network segments).
Implement monitoring controls at these points (e.g. firewall with deep inspection, intrusion prevention system).
Configure rules to detect and block suspicious data transfers (sending large volumes, sending to unusual destinations).
Periodically review the alerts generated and adjust the rules.
evidence: PDF network diagram showing external borders and internal critical points where controls are implemented.
Screenshot of firewall rules or intrusion prevention system configured to detect data exfiltration.
Report or screenshot of alerts generated by the monitoring system in the last 3 months.
PDF record of the revision of the rules, with date and person responsible.
3.tip: Many new generation firewalls (such as Fortinet, Sophos or pfSense) already include deep traffic inspection features. Check if your firewall has these features and if they are active.
3.effort: 8–16 hours | An IT person | Possible Firewall Licensing Cost

PR.SD-4.title: The entity protects the confidentiality, integrity and availability of data in use
PR.SD-4.description: The entity applies measures to protect its systems against attacks that prevent services from functioning (denial of service attacks) or that compromise data while it is being processed.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Implement security controls to protect critical systems or limit the impact of denial-of-service attacks (when someone floods a system with traffic to make it unavailable).
indicators: Identify critical systems exposed to the internet or external networks that could be targets of denial of service attacks.
Implement protection mechanisms against these attacks (e.g. internet provider's anti-DDoS service, CDN with DDoS protection, firewall rate limits).
Configure alerts to detect abnormal traffic spikes.
Test the effectiveness of mechanisms and document results.
evidence: Excel sheet with columns: Critical system | Exhibition (internet/internal) | Protection mechanism applied | Service provider.
Screenshot of the anti-DDoS service configuration or rate limits on the firewall.
Screen print of alerts configured to detect abnormal traffic spikes.
PDF record of the tests carried out, with date, results and person responsible.
3.tip: Most business internet providers offer basic protection against denial-of-service attacks. Confirm with your supplier whether this service is included in the contract.
3.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | Possible cost of anti-DDoS service

PR.SD-5.title: Backups of the entity's data are made, maintained and tested
PR.SD-5.description: The entity makes regular backups of important data, stores them in a separate location, and periodically tests whether it can recover data from those copies.

1.description: Regularly make backup copies of critical data and store them in a system separate from the original.
indicators: Identify critical data that needs backup (work files, database, system configurations, email).
Define the frequency of backups (e.g. daily for data that changes frequently).
Configure automatic backup execution.
Ensure that copies are saved on a system or device other than the one where the original data is (e.g. external disk, separate NAS, cloud service).
Periodically check that the copies are being made successfully.
evidence: Screenshot of the backup tool showing the configuration (data included, frequency, destination).
Screenshot of the last successful backup run report.
Excel sheet with columns: Data copied | Frequency | Copy Destination | Last Checked | State.
1.tip: The 3-2-1 rule is easy to remember: 3 copies of the data, on 2 different media, with 1 copy off-site. Start by ensuring at least one automatic copy daily on a separate disk or server.
1.effort: 2–4 hours | An IT person | Variable cost (external disk or cloud service)

2.description: Define and approve a Backup Policy. Regularly test the integrity of copies. Implement redundancy and store copies in distinct, protected locations.
indicators: Create and approve a Backup Policy that defines: what data is backed up, how often, where, who is responsible, how long it is retained and how it is tested.
Perform periodic restoration tests (recover data from the copy and confirm that it is intact and usable).
Implement a redundancy scheme (e.g. local copy + cloud copy, or copy in two different locations).
Ensure that the off-premises copy has the same level of protection as the local copy (restricted access, encryption).
evidence: Backup Policy in PDF, with sections: scope, data included, frequency, retention, destinations, tests, responsible. Approved and signed by management.
Record of restoration tests carried out, in Excel with columns: Test date | Data restored | Result (success/failure) | Restoration time | Responsible.
Screenshot or diagram showing copy storage locations (local + remote).
Screenshot of the encryption and restricted access configuration applied to backups.
2.tip: The restore test is the most forgotten part. Schedule a quarterly test: choose a random file, restore it from the copy and check if it opens correctly. If you've never tried it, do it today.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | Variable cost (cloud storage)

3.description: The copy verification process must be aligned with the Business Continuity Plan. Copies of critical systems must be separated from copies of information.
indicators: Align the backup verification process with the entity's other plans (Business Continuity Plan, Incident Response Plan).
Physically separate backup copies of critical systems (operating systems, configurations, applications) from copies of information (files, databases).
Store each type of copy in different locations.
Document the separation scheme and validate with management.
evidence: PDF record of the verification process, showing alignment with the Business Continuity Plan and Incident Response Plan (cross-references).
Diagram or Excel sheet showing the separation of copies: Type of copy (system/information) | Content | Storage location A | Storage location B.
Screen print confirming that system and information copies are in separate locations/volumes.
3.tip: The separation between system copies and data copies allows for faster restoration: if the server fails, you can restore first the system and then the data, in parallel if necessary.
3.effort: 4–6 hours | An IT person | No additional cost (if you already have enough storage)

PR.SP-1.title: A base configuration of networks and information systems is created and maintained that incorporates security principles
PR.SP-1.description: The entity defines a secure default configuration for its computers, servers and network equipment, ensuring that they are all configured in a consistent and secure manner.

1.description: Maintain a secure standard configuration of networks and systems that incorporates security principles.
indicators: Define a default configuration for the workstations (allowed programs, operating system security settings, antivirus, active local firewall).
Define a default configuration for the servers (active services, open ports, default accounts disabled).
Define a default configuration for network equipment (routers, switches, Wi-Fi access points: changed passwords, secure protocols).
Record these settings in a reference document.
Apply the default configuration to all new equipment before it is put into production.
evidence: PDF or Excel document with the standard configuration for each type of equipment (workstation, server, network equipment), with fields: Parameter | Configured value | Justification.
Screen print of a device in production showing that the default configuration is applied (e.g. group policy active, firewall turned on).
1.tip: Start with the most basic: confirm that all computers have the Windows firewall active, the antivirus is updated and the default router passwords have been changed. This is already a default setting.
1.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

2.description: Develop, document and maintain a standard security configuration for networks and information systems.
indicators: Formalize the standard configuration document with creation date, version and person responsible for updating.
Include detailed security settings (e.g. list of disabled services, allowed protocols, auditing/logging settings).
Update the default configuration whenever there are significant changes (new type of equipment, new threat, new software version).
Periodically check that production equipment complies with the defined standard configuration.
evidence: Formal standard configuration document (PDF) with version, date, responsible, and detailed sections by system type.
Screen print of compliance checks (e.g. result of a scan or comparison between the actual configuration and the standard configuration).
Record of changes to the document (changelog), with date, change and reason.
2.tip: Use Active Directory group policies (GPO) to automatically apply the default configuration to all computers. This prevents each machine from being configured differently.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

3.description: Configure critical systems with the principle of minimum functionality, disabling unnecessary protocols, ports and services. Implement 'deny all, allow by exception' rules.
indicators: For each critical system, list all active services, ports and protocols and identify which ones are really needed.
Disable everything that is not necessary for the system to function.
Implement the rule 'deny everything by default, allow only what is explicitly authorized' in firewalls.
Document all exceptions (service or authorized port), with justification and approval.
Periodically review active exceptions and remove any that are no longer justified.
evidence: Excel sheet by critical system, with columns: Service/Port/Protocol | Required (Yes/No) | Status (active/disabled) | Justification.
Screenshot of the firewall rules showing the default rule 'deny all' and the authorized exceptions.
PDF record of approved exceptions, with date, justification and signature of the person responsible.
Record of the last review of exceptions, with date and person responsible.
3.tip: In practice, 'deny all, allow by exception' means that the firewall blocks all traffic and then adds rules one by one for what is needed. It's safer than having everything open and blocking it case by case.
3.effort: 8–16 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

PR.SP-2.title: System logs and activity history are documented, implemented and reviewed in accordance with entity Policies
PR.SP-2.description: The entity collects, stores and analyzes the activity records (logs) of its systems in order to detect problems, investigate incidents and maintain a history of what happens on servers and equipment.

1.description: System logs must be collected, stored, protected and reviewed regularly.
indicators: Enable log collection on all relevant systems and equipment (servers, firewall, workstations, critical applications).
Define where the logs will be stored and for how long (e.g. minimum 90 days).
Protect logs against unauthorized alteration and deletion (e.g. restricted access to log folder, copy to separate location).
Review logs regularly (e.g. weekly) to detect relevant events (failed logins, after-hours access, system errors).
evidence: Screenshot of log collection configuration on main systems (e.g. Windows Event Viewer, firewall syslog).
Screenshot showing the log storage location and the configured retention period.
Record of periodic review of logs, in Excel with columns: Date of review | System analyzed | Relevant events found | Action taken | Responsible.
1.tip: On Windows, Event Viewer already collects logs by default. The challenge is to analyze them regularly. Make a habit of reviewing the 'Critical' and 'Error' events once a week.
1.effort: 2–4 hours for setup + 1 hour/week for review | An IT person | No additional cost

2.description: Systems clocks must be synchronized with an official time source so that logs from different systems can be accurately correlated.
indicators: Configure all systems and equipment to synchronize the clock with an official time source (e.g. NTP server of the Lisbon Astronomical Observatory: ntp02.oal.ul.pt, or pool.ntp.org).
Verify that the servers, firewall, switches and workstations are using the same time source.
Confirm that the timestamps in the logs of the different systems are aligned.
evidence: Screen print of the NTP server configuration on each type of system (server, firewall, switch).
Screen print of logs from two different systems showing aligned timestamps for the same event (e.g. a login to the server and the corresponding registration on the firewall).
2.tip: If the systems clocks are not synchronized, when an incident occurs it will be very difficult to understand the correct sequence of events. Configure NTP once and forget about it.
2.effort: 1–2 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

3.description: Generate alerts when log processing fails. Periodically review log storage capacity. Logs from critical systems must be stored in a standalone system.
indicators: Configure automatic alerts for when the log collection service stops or fails (e.g. email alert, notification in the monitoring system).
Configure alerts for when log storage space is almost full.
Periodically review whether the storage capacity is sufficient and increase it when necessary.
Send logs from critical systems to a separate, autonomous system (e.g., dedicated log server or cloud service), so that if the original system is compromised, the logs are not lost.
Restrict access to the autonomous log system to authorized personnel only.
evidence: Screen print of configured alerts for log processing failures and storage capacity.
Screen print of the autonomous log storage system (e.g. syslog server, Graylog, Elastic Stack).
PDF record of the last storage capacity review, with date, status and person responsible.
Screenshot of access permissions to the autonomous log system.
3.tip: Graylog Community Edition is free and allows you to centralize logs from all systems in a single location. All you need is a small server or dedicated virtual machine.
3.effort: 8–16 hours | An IT person | Variable cost (dedicated server or cloud service)

PR.SP-3.title: Installation and execution of unauthorized software is prevented
PR.SP-3.description: The entity blocks the possibility of installing or running programs that have not been approved, to prevent the introduction of viruses, malicious programs or applications that create vulnerabilities in systems.

1.description: Prevent the installation and execution of unauthorized software. Implement network, email and web filters.
indicators: Remove local administration privileges from regular users' workstations (so they can't install software themselves).
Define a process for users to request software installation (formal request to IT).
Implement email filtering to block executable attachments and malicious links (e.g. Microsoft 365 protection, email server spam filter).
Implement a web filter to block access to websites known for distributing malicious software (e.g. firewall configuration, secure DNS such as Quad9).
Configure network filter on the firewall to block traffic from unused protocols.
evidence: Screenshot of a workstation showing that the user does not have local administration privileges.
Screenshot of email filter configuration (e.g. Exchange Online Protection, anti-spam rules).
Screenshot of web filter configuration (e.g. firewall rules, secure DNS configuration).
Screenshot of firewall rules that block traffic from unused protocols.
1.tip: The quickest and most effective measure is to remove local administrator privileges from users. Without these privileges, they cannot install anything, and most viruses cannot install themselves either.
1.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

2.description: Substantial

PR.SP-4.title: A secure software development lifecycle is implemented
PR.SP-4.description: When the entity develops or changes software internally, it applies security rules from conception to production, including security testing before any change is made available.

2.description: The development of systems and applications must incorporate security by design (security by default). Changes and exceptions must be analyzed in a separate test environment before going to production.
indicators: Define security rules to be applied in software development (e.g. validating input data, not storing passwords in text, using parameterized queries for databases).
Require all changes to the software to be tested in a test environment separate from the production environment.
Require a security impact analysis before implementing changes or exceptions in production.
Record all changes made (what, when, who, why).
evidence: PDF document with the security rules applicable to software development, validated by the IT manager.
Screen print of the test environment separate from the production environment (e.g. different servers, different databases).
Excel sheet with record of changes, with columns: Date | Amendment | Impact analysis | Tested in a test environment | Approved by | Implemented into production in.
Example of a security impact analysis performed before a change.
2.tip: Even if the company only has a small internal system, never make changes directly to the environment that users use. Have at least one copy where you test before applying.
2.effort: 6–10 hours | One to two IT people | No additional cost (you can use virtual machines for testing)

3.description: Secure development practices must be integrated throughout the entire software lifecycle and its performance must be monitored.
indicators: Integrate automatic security tests into the development process (e.g. static code analysis, dependency testing with known vulnerabilities).
Define security quality criteria that must be met before any release into production.
Monitor compliance with criteria and record the results of safety tests.
Periodically review secure development practices and update them based on new threats.
evidence: Screenshot of the continuous integration system showing the configured automatic security tests.
PDF document with the security quality criteria required for each release.
Report on the results of the latest release's security tests, with date, findings and corrective actions.
Record of the periodic review of secure development practices, with date and person responsible.
3.tip: Free tools like SonarQube Community Edition or Dependabot (GitHub) automatically analyze code for known vulnerabilities. Integrate them into the development process.
3.effort: 8–16 hours | One to two IT/development people | No additional cost (open source tools)

PR.RI-1.title: The entity protects the integrity of communications networks
PR.RI-1.description: The entity divides its network into separate zones (segmentation) so that if one zone is compromised, the attacker cannot easily access the rest of the network. Firewalls control traffic between these zones.

1.description: Install and configure firewalls on all networks. Segment and segregate the network from critical systems. Securely configure Wi-Fi access points.
indicators: Confirm that there are firewalls installed and active on all network connections (between the internal network and the internet, between network segments).
Review and update firewall rules to ensure only necessary traffic is allowed.
Separate the network of critical systems (servers, database) from the network of workstations and the visitor network (network segmentation).
Configure Wi-Fi access points securely: strong password, WPA3 protocol (or WPA2 as a minimum), network name (SSID) that does not reveal the organization.
Create a separate Wi-Fi network for visitors without access to the internal network.
evidence: Screenshot of the firewall rules showing the active rules and the date of the last update.
Simple network diagram (PDF or image) showing network segments and the position of firewalls.
Screenshot of Wi-Fi access point configuration (security protocol, SSID, separate guest network).
Screenshot showing that the guest Wi-Fi network does not have access to the internal network.
1.tip: Many corporate routers now allow you to create VLANs (separate virtual networks) and a Wi-Fi network for visitors. This separation is the foundation of a secure network and can be done without extra equipment.
1.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost (if the router/switch supports VLANs)

2.description: Protect the network of critical systems by identifying, documenting and controlling connections between components. Monitor communications at internal and external limits.
indicators: Document all connections between components of critical systems (which server communicates with which database, which application accesses which service).
Limit external connections to critical systems to what is strictly necessary.
Implement protection devices (firewall, IDS/IPS) at the external limits and main internal limits of the critical systems network.
Continuously monitor communications at these points and generate alerts for unauthorized access attempts.
Automatically block unauthorized connections to critical systems.
evidence: Detailed network diagram of critical systems (PDF), showing all connections between components and protection devices.
Screen print of settings for blocking unauthorized connections to critical systems.
Screen print of active network monitoring at the boundaries of critical systems (e.g. IDS/IPS alerts, firewall logs).
2.tip: If you don't have the budget for a dedicated IDS/IPS, the pfSense firewall (free) includes Suricata as an intrusion detection system. It can be installed on a virtual machine.
2.effort: 8–16 hours | An IT person | Variable cost (if you do not have a firewall with IDS/IPS)

3.description: Implement authenticated proxy servers for communication between critical systems and external networks. Implement fault tolerance in protection devices. Control and encrypt data flows from critical systems.
indicators: Implement proxy servers that require authentication for traffic between critical systems and external networks.
Implement redundancy in protection devices (firewall in active-passive or active-active mode) so that a failure in a device does not leave the network unprotected.
Monitor and control data flows from critical systems.
Ensure that all information in transit outside critical systems is encrypted, in accordance with the Data Classification and Management Policy.
Register and approve any exceptions to mandatory encryption.
evidence: Screenshot of the proxy server configuration with authentication, showing the rules applied to traffic from critical systems.
Screen print or diagram showing the redundancy of protection devices (failover configured).
Screenshot of monitoring data flows from critical systems.
Screenshot of encryption applied to external communications.
Exceptions Excel sheet, with columns: Communication | Exception reason | Approval | Date.
3.tip: If your firewall supports high availability (HA), enable it. A firewall that fails and lets all traffic pass through unfiltered is worse than having no firewall at all, because it creates a false sense of security.
3.effort: 16–24 hours | One to two IT people | Variable cost (redundant equipment, proxy)

PR.RI-2.title: Development and test environments are separate from production environments
PR.RI-2.description: The entity guarantees that the systems used for software development and testing are completely separate from the systems that users and customers use on a daily basis (production).

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Development and test environments must be isolated from the production environment, both in terms of access and data.
