indicators: Ensure that development and test environments are on servers or networks separate from the production environment.
Ensure that real production data (personal data, customer data) is not used in test environments (use anonymized or fictitious data).
Ensure that the access credentials for the development/test environment are different from those for production.
Document the separation and applicable rules.
evidence: Screenshot showing that the development and production environments are on different servers and/or networks.
Screenshot showing that the test data is fictitious or anonymized (not real production data).
PDF document with the rules for separating environments, validated by the IT manager.
3.tip: Even in a small company, you can separate environments using virtual machines. Never use the production server for testing, as an error can affect all users.
3.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | Variable cost (virtual machine or additional server)

PR.RI-3.title: The entity's technological assets are protected from natural threats
PR.RI-3.description: The entity protects its IT equipment against risks from the physical environment, such as fires, floods, electricity spikes and extreme temperatures.

2.description: Implement emergency and fire safety systems. Monitor environmental conditions that may affect critical systems. Protect infrastructures against electrical changes.
indicators: Install fire detection and fighting systems in the server room (extinguishers suitable for electrical equipment, smoke detectors).
Install UPS (uninterruptible power supply) on critical systems to protect against power surges and power outages.
Install temperature and humidity sensors in the server room and configure alerts when values ​​exceed acceptable limits.
Document emergency procedures for each type of threat (fire, flood, power failure).
evidence: Photograph or screenshot of the fire detection systems and extinguishers in the server room.
Screen print or photo of UPS installed in critical systems.
Screen print of temperature/humidity sensors and configured alerts.
PDF document with emergency procedures, validated by the person responsible.
2.tip: A basic UPS to protect a server costs between €100 and €300 and gives enough time to shut down the system in a controlled manner in the event of a power failure. It is a fundamental investment.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | One person | Variable cost (UPS, sensors, extinguishers)

3.description: Implement fire detection with automatic notification. Periodically test the effectiveness of security controls applied to physical environments.
indicators: Install fire detectors that send automatic notification to designated personnel (e.g. via SMS, email or app).
Define a schedule for periodic testing of physical security controls (test UPS, check fire extinguishers, simulate alarms).
Carry out the tests according to the schedule and record the results.
Correct the flaws identified in the tests.
evidence: Screenshot or photo of the fire detection system with automatic notification configuration.
Test calendar (PDF or Excel) with planned dates.
Excel sheet with test results, with columns: Date | Control tested | Result | Faults identified | Corrective action | Responsible.
3.tip: Test the UPS at least once a year: turn off the power and confirm that the server continues to operate as expected. A UPS that is never tested may fail when you need it most.
3.effort: 2–4 hours per test | One person | No additional cost

PR.RI-4.title: Mechanisms are implemented to meet resilience requirements in adverse situations
PR.RI-4.description: The entity has prepared controls and mechanisms to keep services running (or recover them quickly) when something out of the ordinary happens, such as a serious system failure.

2.description: Implement controls to respond to interruptions in accordance with the Business Continuity Plan. Ensure redundancy and load balancing in critical systems.
indicators: Verify that the Business Continuity Plan defines the maximum acceptable interruption time for each critical system.
Implement redundancy mechanisms in critical systems (e.g. two servers instead of one, RAID on disks, secondary internet connection).
When applicable, configure load balancing (automatic distribution of work between two or more servers) to maintain availability.
Test redundancy mechanisms to confirm that they work when the main system fails.
evidence: Section of the Business Continuity Plan (PDF) that defines the maximum interruption times for critical systems.
Screen print or diagram showing the redundancy mechanisms implemented (e.g. RAID, clustered servers, secondary internet connection).
Record of the last failure test (failover), with columns: Date | System tested | Result | Switching time | Responsible.
2.tip: The simplest and most economical redundancy is to have the server's disks in RAID (mirror). If one disk fails, the other continues to work. Most servers already come with this functionality.
2.effort: 8–16 hours | An IT person | Variable cost (redundant equipment)

PR.RI-5.title: The entity plans adequate resource capacity to ensure the availability of its networks and information systems
PR.RI-5.description: The entity monitors the use of its resources (processor, memory, disk, bandwidth) and plans future needs in advance to prevent systems from becoming slow or unavailable due to lack of capacity.

2.description: Plan and provide adequate capacity for current and future processing of critical systems, networks, telecommunications and storage.
indicators: Monitor the current use of critical systems resources (processor, memory, disk, network bandwidth).
Identify the capacity limits of each resource and define alert values ​​(e.g. alert when the disk reaches 80%).
Estimate future needs based on anticipated company and data growth.
Create a capacity plan with the necessary actions and investments to ensure that resources keep up with growth.
Review the plan at least once a year.
evidence: Screen print of the monitoring system showing current resource usage values ​​(e.g. Zabbix, PRTG, or even the Windows Server Task Manager).
Excel sheet with the capacity plan, with columns: Resource | Total capacity | Current usage (%) | 12 month forecast | Action required | Deadline | Estimated cost.
Record of the last review of the plan, with date and person responsible.
2.tip: PRTG Network Monitor is free for up to 100 sensors and allows you to automatically monitor server disk, memory and network usage, with email alerts.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost (free tools)

DE.MC-1.title: Networks and information systems are monitored to detect potential incidents
DE.MC-1.description: The entity monitors its network traffic and system activity to detect suspicious behavior or unauthorized activity that may indicate a security incident.

1.description: Install, configure and update network traffic monitoring solutions, including firewalls and protection against unauthorized and potentially malicious activity.
indicators: Check that the firewall is installed, active and has updated rules on all network access points.
Enable the firewall logging feature to monitor blocked and allowed traffic.
Install or activate a network protection solution that detects potentially malicious activity (e.g. firewall IDS functionality, if available).
Ensure detection signatures are updated automatically.
Periodically review alerts generated by monitoring.
evidence: Screenshot of the firewall showing that it is active and has updated rules (date of last visible update).
Screenshot of the active logging configuration on the firewall.
Screenshot of the network monitoring/detection solution installed and with updated signatures.
Alert review record, in Excel with columns: Date | Alert | Origin | Action taken | Responsible.
1.tip: If your firewall already has IDS/IPS (intrusion detection/prevention system) features, simply activate them. You don't need to buy extra equipment.
1.effort: 2–4 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

2.description: Automatically monitor, identify and block unauthorized communications (local and remote) to critical networks and systems and remove malicious content.
indicators: Configure automatic blocking rules for unauthorized communications detected on critical systems.
Implement automatic removal mechanisms for malicious content (e.g. automatic quarantine of infected files).
Monitor remote communications to critical systems and block unauthorized access attempts.
Generate alerts for the IT team when automatic blockages occur, for later analysis.
evidence: Screenshot of the automatic blocking rules configured on the firewall or security system.
Screen print of the malicious content quarantine system (e.g. antivirus with active quarantine).
Report of blocking alerts for the last 3 months, with date, type of threat, origin and action taken.
2.tip: A centrally managed antivirus (such as Windows Defender for Business) allows you to see in a single pane whether all workstations are protected and whether any malicious files have been detected and quarantined.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost (tools included in the system)

3.description: Monitor networks continuously to detect events and indicators of potential incidents, and automatically trigger response mechanisms.
indicators: Implement continuous monitoring (24/7) of networks, with automatic detection of suspicious events.
Configure automatic response mechanisms when incident indicators are detected (e.g. automatically isolate an infected workstation, block an attacking IP).
Integrate monitoring with the security event management system (SIEM, if existing).
Periodically review the effectiveness of automatic response mechanisms.
evidence: Screen print of the continuous monitoring system (e.g. SIEM, EDR) showing that it is active 24/7.
Screen print of configured automatic response rules (e.g. automatic isolation of equipment, blocking of IPs).
Report of the last 3 months with incidents automatically detected and response actions taken.
3.tip: EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) solutions like Microsoft Defender for Endpoint allow you to automatically isolate a compromised computer from the network, preventing the threat from spreading.
3.effort: 8–16 hours | An IT person | Variable cost (EDR/SIEM licensing)

DE.MC-2.title: The physical environment is monitored to enable the detection of potential security incidents
DE.MC-2.description: The entity monitors its physical facilities (especially where servers and equipment are located) to detect unauthorized access or conditions that could put security at risk.

2.description: Monitor the physical environment to identify potentially adverse events (unauthorized access, temperature changes, intrusions).
indicators: Implement monitoring mechanisms in restricted access areas (e.g. surveillance cameras, movement sensors, electronic access records).
Implement environmental sensors in the server room (temperature, humidity, water leaks).
Configure automatic alerts when anomalous events are detected (access after hours, temperature above the limit).
Periodically review monitoring records.
evidence: Screen print or photograph of the installed physical monitoring mechanisms.
Screen print of configured alerts for anomalous events.
Record of periodic monitoring review, in Excel with columns: Date | Event detected | Action taken | Responsible.
2.tip: A temperature sensor with email alert costs less than €50 and can prevent the loss of a server due to overheating. Place it next to the server rack.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | One person | Variable cost (cameras, sensors)

3.description: Monitor physical access to critical systems and devices with intrusion alarms, video surveillance and independent monitoring teams.
indicators: Install physical intrusion alarms in areas that house critical systems and devices.
Install surveillance equipment (cameras) aimed at critical systems and devices.
Hire or designate independent monitoring teams to review surveillance and access records.
Ensure that surveillance records are maintained for an appropriate period of time and protected from tampering.
evidence: Screen print or photograph of physical intrusion alarms installed in critical system areas.
Screen print or photograph of surveillance equipment covering critical system areas.
Contract or formal designation of the independent monitoring team.
Monitoring record of independent teams, with date, findings and actions.
3.tip: If you don't have the budget for a 24/7 monitoring team, there are remote surveillance services that monitor cameras and alarms for an affordable monthly cost.
3.effort: 8–16 hours | One to two people | Variable cost (alarms, cameras, monitoring service)

DE.MC-3.title: Employee activity is monitored to detect potential incidents
DE.MC-3.description: The entity monitors employees' digital activities on company systems to detect behavior that may indicate a security incident, always respecting applicable privacy legislation.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: It is a control without maturity levels defined in the QNRCS. Monitoring employee activity must be integrated into the event management process.
indicators: Confirm the legal framework applicable to monitoring employee activities (GDPR, Labor Code).
Inform employees about the existence and scope of monitoring, as required by law.
Activate activity logs in systems that record relevant actions (logins, access to critical files, data transfers).
Integrate these logs into the event management process so they can be correlated with other security events.
Periodically review logs to detect anomalous behavior.
evidence: PDF document with the legal framework and information provided to employees about monitoring.
Screen print of activity logs activated on systems.
Periodic review record, in Excel with columns: Date | System | Events analyzed | Anomalies detected | Action taken.
4.tip: In Portugal, employee monitoring has strict legal limits. Always inform employees and document the scope of monitoring. Do not monitor personal content. If in doubt, consult a legal expert.
4.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person + legal support | No additional cost

DE.MC-4.title: The entity identifies and implements mechanisms for detecting malicious code
DE.MC-4.description: The entity installs and maintains up-to-date protection software at workstations (computers) to detect and block viruses, malware and other threats.

1.description: Install and update monitoring tools at workstations (endpoints) to detect malicious activity.
indicators: Install an antivirus/anti-malware on all workstations and servers (e.g. Windows Defender, which is already included with Windows).
Ensure that virus definition updates are automatic and daily.
Configure full periodic scans (e.g. a weekly full scan outside of working hours).
Check that all jobs are protected (no unjustified exceptions).
Define who receives alerts when malicious activity is detected.
evidence: Screen print of the antivirus management console (or Windows Security on a workstation) showing that it is active and updated.
Screen print showing the list of protected jobs (if using centralized management) or a sample of several jobs.
Screenshot of the automatic periodic analysis configuration.
1.tip: Windows Defender is already included and active in all versions of Windows 10/11. If you haven't installed another antivirus, you probably already have protection. Just check that it is active and updated on all computers.
1.effort: 1–2 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

2.description: Substantial

DE.MC-5.title: The use of unauthorized applications on mobile devices is detected
DE.MC-5.description: The entity is able to detect when unapproved applications are installed on company devices or personal devices used for work (BYOD).

1.description: Define a list of applications approved for use on company equipment. Document exceptions. Define BYOD criteria in the Acceptable Use Policy.
indicators: Create and approve a list of applications authorized for installation on company equipment.
Define a process for requesting exceptions (installation of unlisted applications), with formal approval.

Include in the Acceptable Use Policy the rules for using personal devices at work (BYOD): what type of access is allowed, what applications must be installed (e.g. antivirus), what data can be accessed.
Communicate the rules to all employees.
evidence: Excel sheet with the list of approved applications, with columns: Application | Version | Purpose | Approved by | Date.
Excel sheet with approved exceptions, with columns: Application | User | Justification | Approved by | Date.
Acceptable Use Policy section (PDF) with BYOD criteria. Signed by management.
Email or communication (PDF) sent to employees informing them of the rules.
1.tip: The list of approved applications does not need to have hundreds of entries. Start with the applications that are actually used (e.g. Office, browser, PDF reader, antivirus) and add as necessary.
1.effort: 2–4 hours | An IT person + management | No additional cost

2.description: Include requirements about corporate mobile devices in the Acceptable Use Policy and monitor compliance.
indicators: Update the Acceptable Use Policy to include specific requirements for corporate mobile devices (company phones, tablets).
Define which applications can be installed on corporate mobile devices.
Implement a mechanism for monitoring compliance with requirements (e.g. periodic manual check or device management tool).
Integrate detected events (installation of unauthorized applications) into the event management process.
evidence: Updated section of the Acceptable Use Policy (PDF) with requirements for corporate mobile devices.
Compliance monitoring record, in Excel with columns: Date | Device | Compliance (Yes/No) | Non-conformities detected | Action taken.
2.tip: Microsoft Intune (included in some M365 Business licenses) allows you to manage corporate mobile devices and automatically detect unauthorized applications.
2.effort: 4–6 hours | An IT person | No additional cost (if you have a compatible license)

3.description: Manage software installation centrally. Create automatic alerts for unauthorized software installation and integrate into incident management.
indicators: Implement a centralized software management and distribution tool (e.g. Microsoft Intune, WSUS for updates, PDQ Deploy).
Configure automatic alerts that detect the installation of software not listed as authorized.
Integrate these alerts into the incident management process (each alert must be analyzed and addressed).
Periodically review the alerts generated and adjust the list of authorized software.
evidence: Screenshot of the centralized software management tool showing managed devices.
Screenshot of an alert generated by the installation of unauthorized software.
Record of the integration of alerts into the incident management process, with an example of a case handled.
3.tip: If you don't have the budget for a dedicated tool, you can use Active Directory group policies (GPO) to prevent unlisted software from running (AppLocker, available in Windows Enterprise).
3.effort: 8–16 hours | An IT person | Variable cost (management tool)

DE.MC-6.title: The activities of external service providers are monitored to detect incidents
DE.MC-6.description: The entity monitors the connections and activities of external suppliers and service providers to its systems, to detect unauthorized access or suspicious behavior.

2.description: Monitor and secure all supplier connections to the entity's systems. Ensure that service providers comply with defined security requirements.
indicators: List all suppliers and external entities that have access to the entity's systems, applications or infrastructure.
Monitor all connections from these providers (record who accesses, when, for how long and to which systems).
Implement protections for these connections (e.g. access via dedicated VPN, vendor accounts with restricted permissions).
Periodically assess whether service providers comply with the security requirements defined in contracts or agreements.
Record the results of the assessment and necessary corrective actions.
evidence: Excel sheet with the list of suppliers with access, with columns: Supplier | Accessible system(s) | Type of access | Protection applied.
Screen print of provider access logs (e.g. VPN logs, remote session logs).
Record of the periodic assessment of providers, in Excel with columns: Supplier | Assessment date | Verified requirements | Compliant (Yes/No) | Corrective actions.
2.tip: Never share administrator credentials with vendors. Create specific accounts for each supplier, with the minimum necessary permissions, and deactivate them when the work is finished.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

DE.MC-7.title: Hardware, software, production environments and their data are monitored to detect potentially adverse events
DE.MC-7.description: The entity monitors the integrity of its hardware, software and data in production to detect unauthorized changes, failures or unexpected behavior.

2.description: Hardware, software, production environments and data must be monitored to detect potentially adverse events.
indicators: Implement monitoring in production systems to detect unauthorized changes to software or configurations (e.g. integrity monitoring tool).
Monitor the status of critical systems hardware (disks, power supplies, temperature).
Configure alerts for anomalous events (e.g. unexpected change to a system file, imminent disk failure).
Register and process detected events.
evidence: Screenshot of the production systems monitoring tool, showing the events collected.
Screen print of configured alerts for anomalous events.
Record of detected events and actions taken, in Excel with columns: Date | System | Event | Gravity | Action taken | Responsible.
2.tip: Many servers generate SMART alerts for disks that are about to fail. Enabling these alerts can give you days or weeks of warning before a failure, enough time to replace the disk.
2.effort: 4–8 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

3.description: Check the integrity of critical systems hardware. Include hardware tamper detection in the Incident Response Plan. Implement a detection system that identifies false positives.
indicators: Implement hardware integrity checks for critical systems (e.g. component checks, behind-the-scenes security seals).
Include procedures in the Incident Response Plan for when hardware tampering is detected.
Implement an event detection system that can identify and filter false positives, to improve the quality of alerts.
Periodically review the effectiveness of the detection system and adjust the rules.
evidence: Screenshot or photograph of hardware integrity verification mechanisms (e.g. behind-the-scenes seals, component logs).
Section of the Incident Response Plan (PDF) with procedures for hardware tampering.
Screenshot of the detection system showing the false positive identification functionality.
Record of the review of the system's effectiveness, with date, false positive rate and adjustments made.
3.tip: An adhesive security seal on the back of the server costs pennies and makes any unauthorized opening visible. It is a simple measure of physical integrity verification.
3.effort: 6–12 hours | An IT person | Variable cost (advanced detection system)

DE.AE-1.title: Detected events are analyzed
DE.AE-1.description: The entity analyzes the security events collected to understand which are relevant, where they come from, what they intend to achieve and what methods were used.

2.description: Analyze the information collected about detected events to filter those with potentially relevant impact and identify origins, targets and attack methods.
indicators: Define a process for analyzing collected security events (who analyzes it, how often, what criteria they use to identify relevant events).
For each relevant event, determine: where it came from (origin), what it tried to achieve (target) and what method was used.
Classify events by potential impact level (low, medium, high).
Record the analysis and conclusions.
evidence: PDF document with the event analysis process (who, when, filtering criteria).
Excel sheet with the analyzed events, with columns: Date | Event | Origin | Target | Method | Potential impact | Action taken | Responsible.
2.tip: Don't try to analyze every event. Focus on the ones that your firewall or antivirus classifies as 'high' or 'critical'. The rest can be reviewed weekly as a block.
2.effort: 2–4 hours/week | An IT person | No additional cost

3.description: Implement an automated system for aggregation, correlation and analysis of detected security events (SIEM).
indicators: Implement a SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) system that collects, aggregates and correlates security events from multiple sources.
Configure correlation rules to detect attack patterns (e.g. multiple failed login attempts followed by a successful login).
Configure automatic alerts for the most critical correlated events.
Periodically review and adjust correlation rules to improve detection.
evidence: Screenshot of the SIEM system showing the integrated event sources and active correlation rules.
Screen print of an alert automatically generated from event correlation.
Record of the review of correlation rules, with date, changes and person responsible.
3.tip: Wazuh is a free open source SIEM that can be installed on a Linux server. It is sufficient for an SME and integrates with most operating systems and firewalls.
3.effort: 16–24 hours to implement | An IT person | Variable cost (dedicated server or VM)

DE.AE-2.title: Events are collected and correlated from multiple sources and sensors
DE.AE-2.description: The entity collects security events from different sources (firewall, servers, antivirus, applications) and correlates this information to have a complete view of what is happening in its systems.

1.description: The collection of security events from systems and applications must be active, and this information must be analyzed and preserved through backup copies for a defined period.
indicators: Ensure that the event collection functionality is active on all relevant systems and applications (servers, firewall, antivirus, critical applications).
Define the retention period for events (how long they are saved, e.g. minimum 90 days).
Analyze collected events periodically (at least weekly for critical systems).
Preserve events through backup copies, so that they are not lost in the event of a failure.
evidence: Screen print of event collection configuration for each type of system (server, firewall, antivirus).
Screenshot showing the configured retention period.
Record of periodic analysis, in Excel with columns: Date | System | Events analyzed | Relevant events | Action taken.
Screenshot of the backup copy of preserved events.
1.tip: If you use a Fortinet, Sophos or pfSense firewall, events are saved locally but have limited space. Configure sending to an external log server (syslog) to avoid losing information.
1.effort: 2–4 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

2.description: Substantial

3.description: The automated event analysis system must integrate all sources and sensors of critical assets, including vulnerability tracking and performance statistics.
indicators: Integrate all event sources for critical assets (servers, firewall, switches, antivirus, applications) into the SIEM.
Integrate vulnerability scan results into SIEM for correlation with security events.
Integrate system and application performance statistics (to detect performance anomalies that may indicate an incident).
Configure correlations that cross security events with vulnerability and performance information.
evidence: SIEM screenshot showing all integrated sources (including vulnerability scanner and performance monitoring).
Screenshot of a correlation rule that crosses security events with known vulnerabilities.
Correlation report showing the integration of several sources in the analysis of an event.
3.tip: Integrating the vulnerability scanner with the SIEM allows you to prioritize alerts: an attack on a system with known vulnerabilities is much more critical than the same attack on an updated system.
3.effort: 8–16 hours | An IT person | Variable cost (vulnerability scanner, SIEM)

DE.AE-3.title: The estimated impact and scope of detected adverse events are understood and identified
DE.AE-3.description: When an adverse event is detected, the entity assesses its potential impact on the functioning of the organization and on people, to decide what actions to take.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: The impacts of security events on the functioning of the entity, operations, assets and people must be considered in the risk management process.
indicators: Define criteria to evaluate the impact of security events (e.g. impact on operations, data, customers, reputation).
When an adverse event is detected, apply the criteria to estimate the impact and scope (which systems and people are affected).
Feed the risk management process with information from detected events (update the risk assessment based on real events).
Document each impact assessment and decisions made.
evidence: PDF document with the impact assessment criteria for security events.
Excel sheet with evaluated events, with columns: Date | Event | Estimated impact | Scope | Decision made | Responsible.
Record of the review of the risk management process showing the integration of security events.
3.tip: Think of impact as a simple question: 'If this happens, what stops working?' The answer helps you quickly prioritize.
3.effort: 2–4 hours per event | An IT + management person | No additional cost

DE.AE-4.title: Information about event detections is communicated
DE.AE-4.description: The entity communicates information about detected adverse events to the right people so they can investigate and respond appropriately.

2.description: Adverse event information must be made available to authorized personnel and configured in relevant systems for detection, investigation and response.
indicators: Define who is the personnel authorized to receive information about adverse events (IT team, security officer, management for serious events).
Configure monitoring systems to automatically send alerts to authorized personnel (e.g. via email, SMS, app).
Ensure that event information is available in relevant systems to support the investigation (e.g. antivirus console, firewall dashboard, SIEM).
Document the communication flow (who is notified, how and in what situations).
evidence: PDF document with the adverse event communication flow: type of event | who is notified | notification channel.
Screen print of the configuration of automatic alerts in monitoring systems (recipients, channels).
Screen print showing that event information is accessible to authorized personnel in the systems.
2.tip: Create a dedicated email group (e.g. security-alerts@company.com) where all alerts are sent. This way, even if one person is absent, another team member still sees the alert.
2.effort: 2–4 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

DE.AE-5.title: Cyberthreat information and other contextual information are integrated into event analysis
DE.AE-5.description: The entity uses external information about known threats (cybersecurity information sources, CNCS alerts, etc.) to enrich and improve the analysis of security events it detects in its systems.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: It is a control without maturity levels defined in the QNRCS. The entity must integrate threat information from legitimate external sources into its event analysis.
indicators: Subscribe to sources of information on threats relevant to the sector (e.g. alerts from CNCS, CERT.PT, security bulletins from software manufacturers used).
When a security event is detected, consult information sources to understand whether it is related to a known threat.
When applicable, integrate automatic threat information feeds into monitoring systems.
Use the context gained to prioritize and improve event response.
evidence: List of sources of information on subscribed threats, in Excel with columns: Source | URL | Type (alert/feed/newsletter) | Frequency.
Example of an event analyzed using external threat information (PDF with the analysis and reference to the source consulted).
4.tip: CERT.PT sends free email alerts about relevant threats in Portugal. Subscribe at https://www.cncs.gov.pt/ and share alerts with the IT team.
4.effort: 1–2 hours setup + analysis time per event | An IT person | No additional cost

DE.AE-6.title: Criteria for declaring incidents are defined
DE.AE-6.description: The entity clearly defines the criteria that determine when a security event (or a set of events) becomes considered an incident that requires formal treatment.

1.description: Define the thresholds that determine when an event, or correlation of several events, constitutes a security incident.
indicators: Define which types of individual events should automatically be considered incidents (e.g. confirmed virus infection, unauthorized access to personal data, compromise of administration credentials).
Define which combinations of events should be considered incidents (e.g. 5 failed login attempts followed by a successful login from another country).
Document these criteria in a simple and accessible format for those monitoring the events.
Communicate the criteria to the team responsible for monitoring.
evidence: PDF document with the incident declaration criteria, organized into: Type of event | Condition/Limit | Classification (Incident Yes/No). Validated by the IT manager.
Record of communication of the criteria to the team (email in PDF or meeting minutes).
1.tip: Start with a few clear criteria rather than trying to cover every scenario. It's easier to add new criteria than to manage a huge list that no one consults.
1.effort: 2–4 hours | An IT person | No additional cost

2.description: Substantial

RS.GI-1.title: The Incident Response Plan is executed, in coordination with interested parties, during or after the occurrence of an incident
RS.GI-1.description: When a cybersecurity incident occurs, the organization activates its Incident Response Plan. Each person involved knows what their role is and what they should do, and actions are coordinated with internal and external stakeholders.

1.description: The Incident Response Plan must identify who does what (roles, responsibilities and competencies) and be executed during or immediately after an incident.
indicators: Include in the Incident Response Plan a table with the name, role, responsibility and competence of each person involved in the response.
Identify the minimum skills for each role (for example: who shuts down systems, who contacts the CNCS, who communicates internally).
Execute the Response Plan whenever an incident occurs, recording who did what and when.
evidence: Incident Response Plan section (PDF) with table: Name | Function | Responsibility | Competence | Contact, approved by the IT manager.
Plan execution record (Excel or entry into ticket system) with columns: Date | Incident | Action taken | Responsible | Start time | End time.
1.tip: Create a simple page in the Response Plan with a 'decision tree': if X happens, person Y does Z. Print it and post it next to IT workstations.
1.effort: 3–4 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Incident response actions must be coordinated with stakeholders defined in the Incident Response Plan, including escalation requirements.
indicators: Identify in the Incident Response Plan the internal and external stakeholders that must be involved in the response (for example: IT supplier, CNCS, insurance company, management).
Define escalation rules: for each severity level, indicate who is contacted, within what timeframe and through what channel.
Coordinate response actions with interested parties, recording communications made and decisions taken together.
Review and update the response process after each significant incident, incorporating lessons learned.
evidence: Incident Response Plan section (PDF) with escalation table: Severity level | Stakeholder | Contact period | Communication channel, approved by management.
Incident coordination record (Excel or archived email) with columns: Date | Incident | Interested party contacted | Channel | Decision made | Responsible.
2.tip: Keep a list of emergency contacts (phone and email) of interested parties in an accessible place even without access to the systems, for example printed in a sealed envelope in the safe.
2.effort: 4–6 hours | IT + Management Manager | No additional cost

RS.GI-2.title: Notifications from detection systems are screened and validated
RS.GI-2.description: When a detection system (antivirus, firewall, external vendor, etc.) generates an alert, the organization analyzes that alert to confirm whether it is a real incident or a false alarm, and records the decision made.

2.description: Incident notifications must be triaged (analyzed in order of priority) and validated (confirmed as real or discarded) according to the Incident Response Plan.
indicators: Define in the Incident Response Plan the criteria for triaging received notifications (for example: origin, type of alert, affected system, apparent severity).
Designate who is responsible for receiving and first analyzing each notification.
Validate each notification, confirming whether it is a real incident or a false positive (false alarm), and record the decision taken.
Record all notifications received, including those that are discarded, with the respective justification.
evidence: Excel sheet or ticket system entry with columns: Date/Time | Alert origin | Description | Apparent gravity | Validation (Real/False Positive) | Justification | Responsible for screening.
Section of the Incident Response Plan (PDF) with the defined screening criteria, approved by the IT manager.
2.tip: Even if the company does not have advanced tools, any email alert, antivirus warning, or service notification should be treated as a notification to triage. The important thing is to register and decide, never ignore it.
2.effort: 2–3 hours to define criteria + continuous recording | One person | No additional cost

RS.GI-3.title: Incidents are categorized according to the defined Incident Response Plan
RS.GI-3.description: Each cybersecurity incident is classified by type (for example: malware, phishing, unauthorized access) and severity level, following rules previously defined in the Incident Response Plan.

2.description: Incidents must be categorized by type and severity, using a taxonomy (organized list of categories) included in the Incident Response Plan.
indicators: Define a categorization taxonomy in the Incident Response Plan (incident types: malware, phishing, unauthorized access, service unavailability, data leakage, etc.).
For each type of incident, define severity levels (for example: Low, Medium, High, Critical) with clear classification criteria.
Categorize each incident at the time of validation, using the defined taxonomy.
Record the categorization assigned to each incident.
evidence: Incident Response Plan section (PDF) with incident taxonomy and severity criteria: Type of incident | Description | Low Level | Medium Level | High Level | Critical Level, approved by the IT manager.
Incident record (Excel or ticket system) with columns: Incident ID | Date | Type (taxonomy) | Gravity | Justification of the classification | Responsible.
2.tip: Use the CNCS taxonomy as an initial reference. It is available free of charge and is the same one that will be used in mandatory notifications.
2.effort: 3–4 hours | One person | No additional cost

3.description: There must be specific response procedures for each type of incident in the taxonomy, supported by automated investigation and impact assessment tools.
indicators: Create specific response procedures (step-by-step guides) for each type of incident in the taxonomy (for example: in the case of ransomware, do A, B, C; in the case of phishing, do X, Y, Z).
Implement automated tools to support incident investigation (for example: centralized log analysis tools or endpoint/EDR detection and response solutions).
Configure these tools to automatically assess the impact of validated incidents (e.g. number of affected systems, compromised data).
evidence: Response guides by incident type (PDF), including: Incident type | Response steps | Responsible for each step | Maximum execution time, approved by the IT manager.
Screenshot or report from the automated investigation tool, showing the analysis of a specific incident (with anonymized data if necessary).
3.tip: If you don't have the budget for commercial analytics tools, consider open source solutions like Wazuh, which offers detection and response capabilities suitable for SMBs.
3.effort: 8–16 hours | IT Manager (or external supplier) | Possible cost of tools

RS.GI-4.title: Incidents are escalated or elevated as needed
RS.GI-4.description: When an incident exceeds the capacity of whoever is handling it, or its severity justifies it, it is passed (escalated) to someone with more authority or competence to decide and act.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: There must be an escalation matrix that defines, for each severity level, who the incident is passed to, within what time frame and through what channel.
indicators: Define in the Incident Response Plan an escalation matrix that indicates, for each severity level, to whom the incident should be escalated (for example: Low severity goes to the IT technician; High severity goes to top management; Critical severity involves external entities).
Establish maximum deadlines for escalation at each severity level.
Record each escalation carried out, including the reason and recipient.
Review the escalation matrix whenever there are changes to the organization's structure or after significant incidents.
evidence: Incident Response Plan section (PDF) with escalation matrix: Severity level | Escalation recipient | Maximum term | Communication channel, approved by management.
Escalation record (Excel or ticket system) with columns: Incident ID | Date | Severity level | Escalated to | Reason | Deadline met (Yes/No) | Responsible.
4.tip: Think of escalation as a hospital emergency: the more serious the situation, the faster it reaches someone with more experience or authority to decide.
4.effort: 2–3 hours | One person | No additional cost

RS.GI-5.title: Criteria for initiating incident recovery are applied
RS.GI-5.description: Before starting to recover systems and services affected by an incident, the organization checks whether the necessary conditions are met (for example: the threat is contained, the cause has been identified) and only then authorizes the start of recovery.

2.description: The criteria for starting incident recovery must be previously defined in the Incident Response Plan and verified before proceeding with recovery.
indicators: Define in the Incident Response Plan the criteria that determine when recovery can begin (for example: threat contained, systems isolated, cause identified).
Document who has the authority to declare the start of the recovery phase.
Apply the defined criteria to each incident before starting recovery, recording the end-to-end verification.
Record the decision to initiate recovery, including the justification.
evidence: Section of the Incident Response Plan (PDF) with a list of criteria for starting recovery and indication of who authorizes it, approved by the IT or management manager.
Record per incident (Excel or ticket system) with columns: Incident ID | Date | Verified criteria (checklist) | Authorized by | Justification | Recovery start date/time.
2.tip: Never begin recovering systems before confirming that the threat is contained. Restoring a server while the attacker still has access is like repairing a door while the thief is still inside the house.
2.effort: 2–3 hours | One person | No additional cost

RS.AI-1.title: A forensic analysis is performed to determine what occurred during an incident and its root cause
RS.AI-1.description: After a significant incident, the organization investigates in depth what happened, how it happened and why (forensic analysis), to identify the original cause of the problem and prevent it from recurring.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: The entity must carry out forensic analysis of the collected artefacts to determine the root cause of the incident and ensure the existence of capacity (internal or external) to do so.
indicators: Ensure there is internal capacity or a contract with an external entity to perform forensic analysis when necessary.
Collect and preserve digital artifacts relevant to the incident (disk copies, logs, memory captures) following good evidence preservation practices.
Perform forensic analysis to identify the root cause: how the incident began, what systems were affected, and what data was compromised.
Document the findings of the forensic analysis in a structured report.
evidence: Contract or agreement for the provision of forensic analysis services (PDF), signed by management, or internal document that identifies the available forensic resources and skills.
Forensic Analysis Report (PDF) with sections: Incident Summary | Collected artefacts | Methodology | Chronology of events | Root cause | Recommendations, signed by the responsible analyst.
3.tip: Most SMEs do not have the in-house capacity to do forensic analysis. Identify a specialized company in advance and have a prior agreement so that, in the event of a serious incident, the response is quick.
3.effort: 2–4 hours (preparation) | IT Responsible | Possible cost with external contract

RS.AI-2.title: Actions taken during an investigation are recorded and the integrity and provenance of the records are preserved
RS.AI-2.description: During the investigation of an incident, all actions taken are documented in a 'logbook'. These records are protected so that no one can alter them, ensuring that they remain reliable for future analysis or sharing with authorities.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: All actions taken during the investigation of an incident must be recorded in detail, and the integrity and provenance of these records must be protected.
indicators: Record all actions taken during the investigation of an incident: who did what, when, on what system and why.
Store records in a secure location, with access control, so that they cannot be altered or deleted.
Ensure the traceability of records, identifying the origin of each entry (person, system, tool).
Protect investigation records during and after the incident, keeping them available for possible future analysis or sharing with authorities.
evidence: Investigation diary (Excel or incident management system) with columns: Date/Time | Action taken | System affected | Responsible | Justification | Result, with version control or password protected.
Screenshot of the storage system showing restricted access permissions to investigation records.
3.tip: Treat the investigation log as a 'logbook' of the incident. Even in an Excel sheet, the important thing is that no one changes what has already been written. Protect the file with a password and save a copy in a folder with restricted access.
3.effort: 1–2 hours (define process) + continuous recording during incidents | One person | No additional cost

RS.AI-3.title: Incident data and metadata are collected, and their integrity and provenance are preserved
RS.AI-3.description: The organization collects the incident's technical data (logs, network records, suspicious files) and its metadata (dates, origins, users), and protects it against changes so that it can be used as reliable evidence.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: Incident data and metadata must be collected and stored securely, protecting their authenticity, accuracy and traceability.
indicators: Define what data and metadata should be collected in each incident (for example: system logs, network logs, timestamps, IP addresses, users involved).
Collect and store data and metadata securely, ensuring that they are not altered after collection.
Implement mechanisms to protect data integrity (for example: generating a hash/fingerprint of each file collected, storage on read-only media).
Document the provenance of each dataset: where it was collected, by whom and when.
evidence: List of data and metadata collected per incident (Excel) with columns: Data type | Origin | Collection date/time | Responsible for collection | Integrity Hash (SHA-256) | Storage location.
Screenshot or report showing secure data storage with restricted access.
3.tip: Generating a hash (a 'fingerprint' of the file) is simple: in PowerShell, use the Get-FileHash command to create a unique code that proves the file has not been modified since collection.
3.effort: 2–3 hours (define process) + continuous recording | One person | No additional cost

RS.AI-4.title: The impact of the incident is estimated and validated
RS.AI-4.description: After investigating the incident, the organization assesses the actual damage: how many systems were affected, what data was compromised, how long downtime there was and what financial, operational or legal consequences resulted.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: The result of the incident analysis must be used to evaluate and document the real impact of the incident, validating it with the affected areas.
indicators: After analyzing the incident, assess the real impact on the entity's systems, data and operations (for example: how many systems were affected, what data was compromised, how long there was unavailability).
Classify the impact using pre-defined criteria (for example: financial, operational, reputational, legal/regulatory).
Validate the impact estimate with the affected business areas.
Record the impact assessment and incorporate it into the final incident report.
evidence: Incident impact assessment report (PDF or Excel) with sections: Incident ID | Affected systems | Compromised data | Downtime | Estimated financial impact | Operational impact | Reputational impact | Validated by, signed by the IT manager and management.
Record of communication of the impact assessment to the affected areas (archived email or meeting minutes).
3.tip: No complex calculations required. Estimate the impact in terms that management understands: 'We were without email for 4 hours', 'We lost last month's invoices', 'The data of 200 customers may have been exposed'.
3.effort: 2–4 hours per incident | IT + Management Manager | No additional cost

RS.NC-1.title: Internal and external stakeholders are notified of incidents
RS.NC-1.description: When a cybersecurity incident occurs, the organization informs its staff about what happened and what they should do, and notifies the relevant authorities (such as the CNCS) as required by law.

1.description: The entity must communicate the occurrence of incidents to its employees, in a clear and simple way, and notify the competent authority (CNCS) in accordance with the Cybersecurity Legal Regime.
indicators: Define a simple model for internal incident communication (what happened, what impact it has, what staff should do or avoid doing).
Communicate to staff the occurrence of relevant incidents, using clear language and without unnecessary technical terms.
Notify the competent authority (CNCS) of incidents that meet the mandatory notification criteria set out in the Cybersecurity Legal Regime.
Record communications made (internal and to the CNCS), including date, time and content.
evidence: Internal incident communication template (PDF or email type) with fields: Date | Incident description | Impact | Actions to be taken by staff, approved by the IT manager.
Proof of notification to the CNCS (screen print from the notification platform or email sent) with date and time.
Record of internal communications (archived email or entry into the system) with date, recipients and content.
1.tip: Familiarize yourself with the CNCS notification platform and legal deadlines before having an incident. In a crisis situation there is no time to learn how it works.
1.effort: 2–3 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: The entity must share incident information with relevant external stakeholders within the timeframe defined in the Incident Response Plan.
indicators: Identify in the Incident Response Plan which external stakeholders must be notified for each type and severity of incident (for example: customers, suppliers, sectoral authorities, CNPD).
Define maximum notification deadlines for each interested party, aligned with legal and contractual requirements.
Share incident information with relevant external stakeholders within defined timeframes.
Record each external notification made, including to whom, when and what information was shared.
evidence: Incident Response Plan section (PDF) with table: Type/Severity of incident | External stakeholder | Notification deadline | Channel | Information to share, approved by management.
Record of external notifications (Excel) with columns: Incident ID | Date | Stakeholder | Channel | Deadline met (Yes/No) | Shared information | Responsible.
2.tip: Create a checklist per incident with all parties that need to be notified. In a crisis situation it is easy to forget someone. Having the list ready avoids failures and delays.
2.effort: 3–4 hours | IT + Management Manager | No additional cost

RS.NC-2.title: There is voluntary sharing of information with external stakeholders
RS.NC-2.description: In addition to mandatory notifications, the organization voluntarily shares information about incidents with communities and interest groups in the sector, helping other entities to protect themselves against similar threats.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: The entity voluntarily shares information about incidents with interest groups and cybersecurity communities, for mutual benefit and improving the sector's response times.
indicators: Identify interest groups or cybersecurity information sharing communities relevant to the entity's sector (for example: sectoral groups, communities promoted by the CNCS).
Define what type of information can be shared voluntarily (for example: indicators of compromise, types of attacks suffered, lessons learned), respecting the entity's confidentiality.
Share information voluntarily with these communities after significant incidents, contributing to collective protection.
Register the shares made.
evidence: Internal document (PDF) identifying the sharing communities and the type of information authorized for sharing, approved by management.
Record of voluntary shares (Excel) with columns: Date | Incident (reference) | Recipient Community/Entity | Shared information | Responsible.
4.tip: Sharing information about attacks suffered is not a sign of weakness. On the contrary, it helps the entire sector to protect itself better. Anonymize sensitive data and contribute to collective security.
4.effort: 1–2 hours per share | One person | No additional cost

RS.MI-1.title: Incidents are contained
RS.MI-1.description: When a cybersecurity incident is confirmed, the organization takes immediate action to prevent the issue from spreading or causing further damage (e.g., isolating the affected computer, blocking the compromised account).

2.description: The entity must contain incidents by following defined procedures. Any decision not to contain must be documented and justified.
indicators: Define in the Incident Response Plan the containment procedures for each type of incident (for example: isolating the computer from the network, blocking the compromised account, deactivating the attacked service).
Execute containment actions as soon as the incident is validated and categorized, following defined procedures.
Document all decisions made during containment, including non-containment decisions (when applicable) and their justification.
Confirm that containment was effective before moving toward resolution or recovery.
evidence: Section of the Incident Response Plan (PDF) with containment procedures by type of incident: Type | Containment action | Responsible | Deadline, approved by the IT manager.

Containment record per incident (Excel or ticket system) with columns: Incident ID | Date/Time | Containment action taken | Result | Non-containment decisions (if applicable) | Justification | Responsible.
2.tip: Containment is like turning off the tap when there is a water leak: first you stop the problem from spreading, then you fix it. Disconnecting a computer from the network is almost always the correct first action.
2.effort: 2–3 hours (define procedures) + execution per incident | One person | No additional cost

RS.MI-2.title: Incidents are resolved
RS.MI-2.description: The organization resolves each cybersecurity incident until its conclusion, eliminating the cause of the problem and restoring the situation to normal. Resolution is only declared when specific criteria are met.

2.description: Substantial

3.description: The Incident Response Plan must contain clear criteria for declaring that the resolution of an incident is complete.
indicators: Include in the Incident Response Plan clear criteria to declare the completion of an incident resolution (for example: root cause eliminated, systems restored, verification tests performed successfully).
Apply the defined criteria to each incident before declaring it as resolved.
Record the declaration of completion of the resolution, including who authorized it and based on what criteria.
evidence: Section of the Incident Response Plan (PDF) with criteria for declaring resolution complete: Criteria | Description | Necessary verification, approved by the IT manager.
Record of completion per incident (Excel or ticket system) with columns: Incident ID | Resolution date | Verified criteria | Authorized by | Observations.
3.tip: Don't declare an incident resolved just because 'everything seems fine'. Use the criteria checklist to confirm each point. It's the difference between 'I think it's resolved' and 'I confirm it's resolved'.
3.effort: 2–3 hours | One person | No additional cost

RC.PR-1.title: The entity follows a recovery plan during or after an incident
RC.PR-1.description: When an incident affects the organization's systems or services, recovery is not done 'head on'. Written procedures are followed, aligned with the Incident Response Plan, to restore everything in an organized and controlled manner.

1.description: There must be incident recovery procedures, integrated into the Incident Response Plan, that are executed and whose actions are recorded.
indicators: Develop incident recovery procedures, aligned with the Incident Response Plan (for example: restore backups, reinstall systems, reconfigure services).
Carry out recovery procedures whenever a justifying incident occurs.
Record all recovery actions taken, including what was done, by whom and when.
evidence: Incident recovery procedures (PDF) with numbered steps: Step | Action | System/Service | Responsible | Prerequisites, approved by the IT manager.
Record of recovery actions per incident (Excel or ticket system) with columns: Incident ID | Date/Time | Recovery action | System/Service | Responsible | Result.
1.tip: Even if it's just 'restore the server backup and check if it works', write down the steps. In stressful situations, having a simple script avoids mistakes and saves time.
1.effort: 3–4 hours | One person | No additional cost

2.description: Substantial

RC.PR-2.title: Recovery actions are defined, prioritized and executed
RC.PR-2.description: In the event of an incident, the organization lists all the actions necessary to recover, orders them by importance (first what is most critical for the business) and executes them in that order, monitoring progress.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: Recovery actions must be listed, prioritized based on the impact on critical services and executed in an organized and monitored manner.
indicators: For each incident, list all required recovery actions (e.g. restore database, reconfigure firewall, update compromised credentials).
Prioritize actions based on the impact on the entity's critical services (recover first what is most important for the organization's functioning).
Execute actions in the defined order of priority, monitoring progress.
Record the execution and result of each action.
evidence: List of recovery actions per incident (Excel) with columns: Action | System/Service | Priority (1=Critical, 2=High, 3=Normal) | Responsible | Status (Pending/Ongoing/Completed) | Completion date, signed by the IT manager.
Execution monitoring record, updating the status of each action.
4.tip: The priority should be simple: first what affects customers or billing, then what affects internal processes, and finally what is ancillary.
4.effort: 2–3 hours per incident | One person | No additional cost

RC.PR-3.title: The integrity of backups and other restore assets is verified before use
RC.PR-3.description: Before using a backup or other resource to restore systems after an incident, the organization verifies that these resources are healthy, not corrupted, and do not contain malicious software.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: Before using backups or other restoration assets, their integrity and authenticity must be verified to avoid reintroducing problems into systems.
indicators: Before using a backup copy for restoration, check that it is intact (not corrupted, does not contain malicious software, corresponds to the expected version).
Test the backup in an isolated environment before applying it to production systems, whenever possible.
Check other restore assets (system images, saved settings) in the same way.
Record the scan result before proceeding with the restore.
evidence: Backup verification log by incident (Excel) with columns: Date | Backup used | Origin | Copy date | Integrity check (Yes/No) | Test in an isolated environment (Yes/No) | Result | Responsible.
Screenshot of the backup tool showing the result of the integrity check.
4.tip: Don't blindly trust backups. A backup infected with the same malicious software that caused the incident will reintroduce the problem. Always check before restoring.
4.effort: 1–2 hours per scan | One person | No additional cost

RC.PR-4.title: The entity considers its critical services and cybersecurity risk management to establish post-incident operational standards
RC.PR-4.description: After recovering from an incident, the organization analyzes what happened and decides whether it needs to change the way it operates its most important services, adjusting rules and procedures to reduce the risk of the problem recurring.

2.description: Substantial

4.description: Post-incident findings should be used to reassess cybersecurity risks and, if necessary, define new operational rules for critical services.
indicators: After recovering from an incident, analyze whether the operational practices of critical services need to be adjusted in light of what happened (for example: strengthening configurations, changing permissions, restricting access).
Reevaluate cybersecurity risks in light of the incident that occurred, identifying new vulnerabilities or threats.
Define and implement post-incident operational standards that reflect necessary adjustments.
Communicate new standards to affected personnel.
evidence: Post-incident report (PDF) with section dedicated to: Critical services affected | Recommended operational changes | New risks identified | Updated operational standards, approved by management.
Record of communication of new standards to affected personnel (archived email or meeting minutes).
4.tip: An incident is always an opportunity to improve. Ask: 'What can we change so this doesn't happen again?' and record the response as a new operational standard.
4.effort: 3–4 hours | IT + Management Manager | No additional cost

RC.PR-5.title: The integrity of restored assets is verified, systems and services are restored, and normal operating status is confirmed
RC.PR-5.description: After restoring systems and services affected by an incident, the organization verifies that everything is working correctly before declaring that the situation is back to normal. It’s not enough to turn on the systems: you need to test and confirm.

2.description: The integrity of restored assets must be verified, systems and services must be recovered and their normal functioning must be confirmed before considering them operational.
indicators: After restoring each asset (server, application, database), check its integrity (correct data, settings applied, no signs of compromise).
Restore systems and services affected by the incident, following recovery procedures.
Confirm the normal operating status of each system and restored service (for example: access tests, log checks, confirmation with users).
Record verification and confirmation of operation for each restored asset.
evidence: Verification and restoration log (Excel or ticket system) with columns: Asset restored | Restoration date | Integrity check (Yes/No) | Tests carried out | Normal operation confirmed (Yes/No) | Confirmed by | Confirmation date.
Screen print or test report to verify normal operation (for example: system access, execution of critical processes).
2.tip: Don't just turn on the system and declare that it works. Have a real user test the most important functions before confirming that everything is operational.
2.effort: 2–4 hours per system | IT Responsible | No additional cost

RC.PR-6.title: End of incident recovery is documented and declared based on pre-defined criteria
RC.PR-6.description: Recovery from an incident is only officially completed when all previously defined criteria have been met (for example: systems restored, tests performed, communications completed). This closure is documented and signed.

2.description: The end of recovery must be formally documented and declared only when pre-defined criteria are fully met.
indicators: Define clear criteria for declaring the end of recovery from an incident (e.g., all systems restored, normal functioning confirmed, no evidence of residual malicious activity, communications completed).
Verify compliance with all defined criteria before declaring the end of recovery.
Formally document the closure of recovery, indicating when, by whom and based on what criteria.
Archive all incident documentation, from detection to completion of recovery.
evidence: List of recovery termination criteria (PDF) with: Criteria | Description | Necessary evidence, approved by the IT manager.
Incident recovery closure report (PDF or Excel) with fields: Incident ID | Closing date | Verified criteria (checklist) | Stated by | Signature, signed by the IT manager and validated by management.
2.tip: Treat the end of the recovery like the end of a project: it is only completed when all points on the checklist are verified and signed.
2.effort: 1–2 hours per incident | One person | No additional cost

RC.CO-1.title: Recovery activities are communicated to interested parties, as well as management, management and administration bodies
RC.CO-1.description: During and after recovery from an incident, the organization keeps stakeholders (internal and external) and top management informed about progress: which systems have already been restored, which are still in process, and what is expected to be completed.

2.description: Recovery activities and progress in restoring normal operation must be communicated to internal and external stakeholders and senior management.
indicators: Define who should be informed about recovery activities and restoration progress (internal and external stakeholders, top management).
Regularly communicate the status of the recovery: which systems have already been restored, which are still in process and what is expected to be completed.
Record all communications made during the recovery process.
evidence: Record of recovery communications (Excel or archived email) with columns: Date | Recipient | Channel | Summary content (recovery status) | Responsible for communication.
Email or progress report sent to stakeholders and management, with information on: Systems restored | Outstanding Systems | Completion forecast.
2.tip: Send short, regular updates, even if they're 'no news yet'. Silence during a crisis is worse than an update with no progress.
2.effort: 1–2 hours per communication cycle | One person | No additional cost

RC.CO-2.title: Incident recovery updates are shared using entity-approved methods
RC.CO-2.description: Public communications about recovery from an incident (to customers, suppliers, press) are only made through channels previously approved by management, to ensure that the message is controlled and does not cause additional damage to the organization's reputation.

2.description: Public updates on incident recovery must be shared exclusively through previously defined communication methods approved by management.
indicators: Define and internally approve the communication methods to be used to share public updates on incident recovery (for example: announcement on the company website, email to customers, press release).
Ensure that all public updates undergo management approval before being released.
Use only approved methods to share public information about recovery.
Record all public communications made.
evidence: Document with approved communication methods for public updates (PDF), including: Channel | Message type | Who approves | Deadline, approved by management.
Record of public recovery updates (Excel) with columns: Date | Channel used | Summary content | Approved by | Responsible for publicity.
2.tip: Never post information about an incident on social media or the website without management approval. Poor communication can cause more damage to reputation than the incident itself.
2.effort: 2–3 hours | IT + Management Manager | No additional cost

3.description: The entity must have a documented and approved crisis communication strategy, which defines procedures, spokesperson, standard messages and priority channels for cybersecurity crisis situations.
indicators: Develop a documented and approved crisis communications strategy that defines communications procedures during a cybersecurity crisis.

Include in the strategy: designated spokesperson, standard messages for different scenarios, priority communication channels, rules for managing social networks and procedures for dealing with questions from journalists.
Test the crisis communications strategy periodically (for example, as part of incident simulation exercises).
evidence: Crisis Communication Strategy (PDF) with sections: Designated Spokesperson | Typical messages per scenario | Priority channels | Rules for social networks | Media procedures | Emergency contacts, approved by top management.
Record of test or exercise of the communication strategy (meeting minutes or exercise report) with date, participants and lessons learned.
3.tip: Prepare two or three standard messages for the most likely scenarios: 'We have had a technical problem and are resolving it', 'Customer data has not been affected'. Having these messages ready saves hours of indecision.
3.effort: 4–6 hours | Management + Communication | No additional cost
